data.title: GPT Image empowers Seedance 2.0: Everyone can shoot Hollywood blockbusters.
data.description:
Author: Changan, Biteye Content Team

Just after the Spring Festival in 2026, actors in Hengdian started posting videos on Douyin complaining about not getting any work.

"The crew's group chat is quiet. Before, announcements would come in before the Lantern Festival, but this year, by the end of February, not a single one."

That Spring Festival, ByteDance's video generation model, Seedance 2.0, quietly launched, sweeping across the entire short drama industry.

This article aims to clarify three things:

  • What happened to this industry after Seedance 2.0?

  • How exactly are AI short dramas created?

  • And what opportunities does this mean for ordinary people? First, a model that changed the entire industry. During the Spring Festival, ByteDance's SeeDance 2.0 video generation model was officially launched. Tim from Filmstorm said "terrifying" six times in a test video. It reshaped the entire video industry from the production side: you don't need a camera crew, actors, or locations; a text description, a reference image, and you can generate a publishable video in minutes. With the barrier to entry lowered, two types of needs that were difficult to meet in the past have been released. Turning impossible scenes into videos: Enabling creative adaptations of films and television shows, such as "Have you ever saved a fox at the foot of the Tianshan Mountains?" Scenes we want to see but can't: These are fundamental emotional needs; some scenes may never have been filmed again, but AI gives them a chance to exist. These two examples together illustrate the same point: the emergence of AI video generation tools has changed the way we use video as a medium. It's no longer the exclusive domain of professional teams and equipment, but has become something that every ordinary person can use to express themselves, convey emotions, or even simply for entertainment. This capability has led to an explosion of two types of video content.

    1️⃣Short video content centered on entertainment and traffic

    This type of content is not as complex as short dramas. It doesn't require considering the consistency of characters across multiple videos, nor does it need to maintain a continuous storyline. Essentially, it strips away some tedious and repetitive tasks and lets AI do them.

    The most typical example is AI digital voiceover. The process is simple: upload a photo of yourself to generate a digital avatar, write the text, and AI automatically completes lip-syncing, voice-over, and image output.

    Another type is the visualization of jokes. Many purely text-based jokes circulating online have humor but lack visuals, limiting their reach. Now, some people are specifically converting these jokes into videos, adding subtitles and voice-overs, turning a text-based joke into a short video.

    ...

    2️⃣ AI Short Dramas Centered on Plot

    Short dramas are far more complex than short videos because the plot is continuous. The same character must appear from the first episode to the sixtieth, with their face, clothing, and scene style remaining consistent. This requirement for consistency increases the workflow difficulty by an order of magnitude.

    Due to ByteDance's restrictions on real-person images, many creators have turned to directions that do not require real faces—animated dramas.

    Animated dramas use AI-generated animated characters to replace real people, circumventing compliance issues and simultaneously opening another door: adapting online novel IPs. Fantasy, underdog stories, and system-based narratives—these themes, with hundreds of millions of readers on platforms like Tomato and Qidian—are naturally suitable for making animated short dramas.

    II. From Script to Finished Product: The Complete Workflow of an AI Short Drama

    Many people see a video and assume that it's just inputting a plot description and the model automatically generates it.

    That's not actually the case. A high-quality AI short drama follows a well-defined workflow, with corresponding tools for each step, and the quality of each step directly affects the final product.

    Step 1: Writing the Storyboard

    The script requires you to clearly write out every shot. The format is similar to this: Shot 3, kitchen, close-up, the male lead takes ingredients out of the refrigerator, the camera moves from his hand to his face, his expression is tired, duration 5 seconds, voiceover "It's that time of the year again."

    The more detailed this step is, the more stable the subsequent generation will be.

    AI models understand explicit visual instructions, not vague narratives. A well-written storyboard reduces randomness in subsequent steps. The second step: Establishing a reference image library for characters and scenes. This is the most easily overlooked yet crucial step in the entire process. The biggest problem with AI-generated videos isn't image quality, but consistency. The same character might have a different face in one episode than the next. Background colors will shift, and clothing details will disappear. Without fixed reference images, it's almost impossible to produce series with more than three episodes. The solution is to use a dedicated image-generating tool to "finalize" the characters before officially generating the video—generating one image each from the front, side, three-quarter angle, and eyes, fixing hair color, skin tone, clothing, and style. The same process is applied to major scenes. This image library will be referenced in all subsequent shots and forms the foundation of the entire workflow. ⚠️A little trick: If you want to generate a live-action video using JiDream, you can blur the eyes in a person's face photo and add the caption "This person was generated by AI" to the image. The eyes should be displayed separately in the image. This can bypass the platform's face detection restrictions. Step 3: Control the card-drawing rate using the first frame Anyone who has worked on AI videos knows the term "card drawing." What is the probability that the generated video result is directly usable? High-quality prompts and reference images can significantly reduce the number of card draws. Professional teams first use a raw image tool to generate the first frame of each shot, then use this image as reference input, allowing Seedance to generate subsequent animations starting from this frame. In this step, the quality of the raw image tool directly determines the ceiling of the final video. The better the generated image and the more consistent the details, the better the resulting video will be when fed into Seedance. This is also why GPT Image 2 has had such a significant impact on the entire industry. GPT Image 2's ability to understand scene descriptions has leaped to a new level; it can generate high-quality reference images from a scene description, with more stable faces and more controllable styles. Improved reference image quality leads to improved quality in all downstream stages of the final product—it's a chain reaction. Fourth step: Editing and compositing. After the segments are confirmed, they are spliced ​​together using CapCut or other editing tools, and subtitles, voiceovers, and background music are added. Seedance 2.0 supports generating sound effects and background music simultaneously with video generation, and the lip-sync and audio synchronization are quite stable. This feature can save a considerable amount of post-production work. III. Traditional Short Dramas vs. AI Short Dramas: An Unequal War Having discussed the process, what about the cost? How much does a 60-episode AI short drama actually cost? How does it compare to traditional live-action short dramas? Standard Membership: 199 RMB per month, including 2210 points, which can generate approximately 200 seconds of video, translating to a cost of about 1 RMB per second. Premium Membership: 499 RMB per month, including 6160 points, which can generate approximately 560 seconds of video, reducing the cost to around 0.89 RMB per second. However, this price isn't always the case. This year, Jimo has raised its prices multiple times. The original annual membership was priced at 2599 yuan, equivalent to 216 yuan per month, and included 15,000 points per month. Now, the annual membership has increased to 5199 yuan, and in April of this year, the monthly points were slashed from 15,000 to 6160, a reduction of over 60%. This is equivalent to a reduction of more than half in the video length that can be generated with the same budget, increasing the actual cost by 60%. At Jimo, generating 1 second of video consumes 11 points. Assuming a short episode is 1 minute long, without any card draws, the actual cost of one short video episode is approximately 46 yuan. The card-drawing rate varies greatly depending on the quality of the prompts and the complexity of the scene. Assuming an average of 4 generation attempts are needed per video to obtain a usable segment, the actual computing cost per episode of a short drama is approximately 184 yuan. This is under the premise of stable prompt quality and relatively simple scenes. If the plot is complex and the characters' movements are large, the number of card draws will only increase. Beyond computing power, there are also operating costs. A small AI short drama team typically consists of 3 to 5 people, including writers, card drawers, and editors. Monthly fixed expenses, including personnel, office rent, and utilities, range from 35,000 to 70,000 yuan. Distributed among 10 dramas produced per month, the comprehensive cost per episode, including operating costs, is approximately less than 500 yuan. Traditional live-action short dramas are divided into male-oriented and female-oriented genres, with significant cost differences. Male-oriented dramas: These feature more action scenes and special effects. A 60-episode series typically costs over 500,000 yuan to produce, translating to approximately 8,300 yuan per episode. Female-oriented dramas: These focus on emotional storylines, with relatively controllable costs. A 60-episode series costs between 350,000 and 400,000 yuan, or about 5,800 to 6,700 yuan per episode. In contrast, even with the added team operating costs, the total cost of an AI short drama episode is no more than 500 yuan. The cost difference between traditional live-action production and AI production for a single episode is between 15 and 40 times. This gap means that a traditional short drama requires a bet of hundreds of thousands of yuan; a wrong topic selection can be a serious blow, and the entire team may need months to recover. AI short dramas cost only a few hundred yuan per episode, and the same budget can be used to develop ten topics simultaneously, trading quantity for probability and speed for a window of opportunity. Fourth, what does this mean for ordinary people, and is there an opportunity? In 2025, the Chinese micro-drama market reached 67.79 billion yuan, with a user base of 696 million, meaning more than half of Chinese netizens watch short dramas. This is the fertile ground for AI short dramas; there's no need to cultivate a new market, as AI short dramas have already established a stable paid subscription habit. Based on this, Douyin (TikTok) has also begun to actively drive traffic and funding towards AI original videos. Douyin, in partnership with JiMeng, launched the "AI Creation Wave Plan S2": Ten high-quality pieces of content are evaluated every two weeks, with regular content receiving a cash reward of 1500 yuan. Top-ranked creators also receive priority access to industry collaborations, commercial projects, and short film project applications. Driven by platform incentives, this month's Douyin creation boom has produced a batch of content of significantly higher quality than before. AI-powered public service short dramas such as "Paper Airplane," "Centenarian Kindergarten," and "Farewell" have generally garnered high numbers of likes. The monetization path is also straightforward: domestic creators can pursue three parallel avenues: platform traffic revenue sharing, Tomato Novels CPS commissions, and brand commercial projects.

    • For accounts with 10,000 to 50,000 followers, the price per commercial post is 500 to 2,000 yuan;

    • For accounts with 50,000 to 100,000 followers, the price per post is 2,000 to 5,000 yuan;

    • For accounts with 100,000 to 500,000 followers, the price per post can reach 5,000 to 20,000 yuan.

    The platform also has a traffic revenue sharing program. Douyin's mid-video program offers approximately 60 yuan per 10,000 views, and Kuaishou's Magnet Star program offers approximately 40 yuan per 10,000 views; the entry requirements are not high.

The platform also has a traffic revenue sharing program. Douyin's mid-video program offers approximately 60 yuan per 10,000 views, and Kuaishou's Magnet Star program offers approximately 40 yuan per 10,000 views; the entry requirements are not high.

Game promotion, app user acquisition, and brand integration—advertisers are already heavily investing in short drama content; AI short dramas simply offer them a lower-cost option. In conclusion, given the large market, who is suitable to enter? Those without any background should not jump into short dramas. Short dramas demand high consistency in characters and scenes, have complex workflows, and incur significant trial-and-error costs. A more pragmatic approach is to start with short videos. Douyin (TikTok) has many videos that convert popular online phrases into content. These videos don't require a continuous storyline or fixed characters; each video is independent. These accounts gain followers quickly and have high view counts, making them suitable for building a personal brand and audience base. More importantly, these types of videos almost don't require consideration of character consistency or scene consistency, allowing users to focus entirely on content selection and pacing. Once the account is up and running and the user has a feel for the tools and platform rules, then the content quality can be improved, gradually exploring more complex short drama workflows. The AI ​​short drama market hasn't yet seen a true monopolist; tools are iterating, workflows are evolving, and a team that works with one process today might be overtaken by a better model tomorrow. This means the first-mover advantage isn't as significant as imagined, and latecomers still have a chance.

data.title: How can we reduce the risk of address poisoning at the source?
data.description:
Written by: imToken

In the Web3 world, many people's first reaction to security is to protect their private keys, mnemonic phrases, and authorization permissions.

These are certainly important, but in actual use, there is another type of risk that doesn't come from private key leaks or rely on contract vulnerabilities, but occurs in a seemingly ordinary operation: address copying

Address poisoning exploits this. It doesn't profit by cracking the system, but by disguising, interfering with, and inducing users to transfer assets to incorrect addresses in a seemingly normal transaction process.

The reason this type of attack is so difficult to detect is not because of its high technical threshold, but because it precisely exploits users' visual habits and path dependencies in daily operations.

What is address poisoning?

Address poisoning refers to an attacker generating a disguised address that is visually highly similar to a user's frequently used address, and then mixing this address into the user's transaction history through zero-amount or extremely small transactions. When the user needs to transfer funds again, if they "casually copy" the address from historical transactions without verifying the complete characters, they may mistakenly transfer assets to the disguised address prepared by the attacker. This type of attack is not uncommon. In the past two years, several public cases have emerged on-chain, proving that address poisoning not only causes actual losses, but even the habitual practice of "testing with small amounts before making formal transfers" may not be sufficient to avoid the risk. Even more serious is that due to the significant reduction in gas fees caused by the Fusaka upgrade, the marginal cost of address poisoning attacks has indirectly decreased significantly. According to BlockAid statistics, there were 3.4 million on-chain poisoning attempts in January 2026, a 5.5-fold increase compared to November of the previous year (628,000 attempts), showing an explosive growth in poisoning frequency. Why is address poisoning so easy to fall victim to? From a theoretical perspective, address poisoning is not complicated; what makes it truly difficult to defend against is that it exploits several inherent weaknesses in user operations. 1. Addresses themselves are not suitable for manual verification. A typical on-chain address consists of 42 characters. For most users, verifying the complete address character by character is not a realistic, stable, or sustainable approach. Often, people only look at the first and last few characters, confirming "it looks like that address" before moving on. Attackers design their disguises precisely around this habit. 2. Malicious transactions can blend into normal transaction noise. Address poisoning transactions often occur with extremely low amounts or even zero amounts, and are essentially no different from ordinary on-chain transfers. When they are mixed into real transaction records, it is difficult for users to quickly distinguish which transactions are normal and which are deliberately placed interference items from a long list of historical records with the naked eye. 3. Traditional alerts often appear too late. Many security alerts occur before "confirming the transfer." However, for address poisoning, the truly critical risk point is usually much earlier—the moment the user decides to copy the address from the history. If risk identification and alerts only appear in the last step, then the path of erroneous operation has already been formed.

Faced with address poisoning, wallets need to do more than just "remind."

The special thing about this type of risk is that it cannot be completely solved simply by users taking a closer look or being more cautious.

As the entry point for users to interact with the blockchain, wallets should take on more proactive judgment and protection work, stopping risks at earlier points of contact, rather than leaving all the pressure on users.

In imToken 2.19.0, we have further upgraded our security risk control capabilities for address poisoning-related risks. The overall approach is not to add a single prompt, but to move identification, filtering, reminders, and verification to a more appropriate position in the actual user operation process.

Three-layer protection against address poisoning

1. Hide high-risk transactions to reduce bill contamination

The new version has enabled the "Hide Risk Transactions" feature by default to prevent malicious addresses from contaminating bill records through small or zero-amount transactions.

When the system identifies a high-risk poisoning transaction, it will prioritize filtering it in transaction records and related notifications to minimize the amount of such interfering information that directly enters the user's view.

The purpose of this is not only to make the interface cleaner, but more importantly, to reduce the probability of users accidentally copying risky addresses from the history.

2. Proactively alert users when the copying action occurs

The most critical breakthrough point for address poisoning is not the transfer button itself, but the step of copying the address.

Therefore, when a user performs a copy operation from the transaction details page, the system will add clearer interactive prompts to guide the user to perform a more complete verification of the address, rather than relying solely on the first and last characters for judgment. Compared to only providing prompts before the transfer, this method is closer to the point where real risks occur and is more helpful in breaking the habitual path of "casual copying." 3. Continuously Marking Risks in Key Links In addition to the record list and copying scenarios, the system will also provide clear markings and corresponding reminders for suspected risk addresses at key touchpoints such as transaction details and pre-transfer verification. This is not to increase disturbance, but to provide more timely and consistent risk feedback before the user actually takes the next step. **Technical Explanation: Why Address Poisoning Requires Dynamic Risk Control** Address poisoning doesn't exploit on-chain protocol vulnerabilities, but rather leverages users' operational habits and visual inertia. Attackers create fake addresses highly similar to real addresses and then mix them into historical records with small or zero-value transactions, inducing users to mistakenly copy and transfer them in subsequent operations. **The difficulty in controlling this is largely due to the fact that, from the perspective of on-chain execution results, these transactions are often "normal." There are no obvious protocol anomalies or traditional attack signatures, so simply relying on static blacklists or post-incident alerts is often insufficient to cover the real risks.** ImToken's approach to this type of risk isn't simply about permanently labeling addresses as "good" or "bad." Instead, it dynamically identifies suspicious transactions at key touchpoints such as when users refresh transaction records, view details, copy addresses, or initiate transfers, combining real-time on-chain data and the current interaction context. This drives the client to perform actions such as filtering, marking, strong alerts, or pre-verification. Risk identification isn't just about "similarity." The key to poisoning detection isn't just whether the strings are similar, but how to combine multiple types of evidence for judgment in a complex and noisy environment. The current identification logic mainly considers the following types of signals: For an attack to succeed, the spoofed address must first be visually "sufficiently similar." The system quantifies the structural features of address spoofing to identify this type of high-similarity risk. **Cost-Based Evidence** Address poisoning, in order to spread at low cost, often exhibits specific monetary distributions and transaction patterns. While monetary signals themselves are not decisive, they can be used in conjunction with other evidence to reduce misjudgments caused by single factors. **Behavioral Timing Evidence** Some poisoning transactions closely follow a user's actual transfer behavior, attempting to leverage the user's post-transaction momentum to quickly insert a fake address into the transaction record. The system will comprehensively assess such behavior within a specific time window and context. **Why Make a Unified Risk Decision?** A single signal is often insufficient to support a highly reliable risk assessment. Therefore, the system will comprehensively evaluate multiple types of evidence, output a unified risk result, and then map it to processing strategies at different touchpoints. This design primarily brings three benefits: Reduced false alarm noise: Weak signals will not trigger high-level actions independently. Ensure consistent user experience: The same transaction receives consistent risk assessment across different pages. Support for review and optimization: The basis for judgment can be traced back after each hit, facilitating continuous iteration. This type of risk control capability is particularly challenging for non-custodial wallets. This is because address poisoning exploits user behavior paths, rather than obvious on-chain anomalies; and the attack methods continuously change with the chain, assets, pace, and disguise methods. In the absence of a centralized control point, the effectiveness of protection relies more heavily on the synergy between identification quality, product touchpoint design, and policy iteration capabilities. Therefore, imToken has built these capabilities into a continuously evolving security engineering system, supporting policy updates, version management, and effect observation and review, ensuring that protection capabilities can continuously keep pace with changes in attack methods. If you are already using imToken, we recommend upgrading to version 2.19.0 as soon as possible. Regarding address poisoning risks, the new version has the corresponding protection capabilities enabled by default, requiring no additional settings to provide a more proactive risk identification and alert experience. In conclusion, address poisoning reminds us that Web3 security doesn't only occur at the "most dangerous" moments; it can also lurk in the most everyday and familiar operations. When risks begin to exploit people's habits, security capabilities need to move beyond "outcome alerts" to "process protection." For wallets, it's more important than simply executing transactions; it's about helping users reduce misjudgments and mitigate the risk of erroneous operations at critical junctures. This is why imToken continuously upgrades its security risk control capabilities: to allow users to maintain self-control while receiving more timely and practical security protection.

data.title: You haven't bought rsETH, but your WETH is frozen.
data.description:
Introduction

Key Points:

  • Four attack incidents were detected between April 13th and April 19th, 2026, involving multiple chains including Ethereum, Unichain, Arbitrum, and NEAR, with a total estimated loss of approximately $310M.

  • Attack vectors include RPC infrastructure poisoning against LayerZero DVN, MMR proof forgery, signed integer abuse in the insurance fund, and circular swap path exploitation in the margin trading protocol.

  • The attack vectors include RPC infrastructure poisoning against LayerZero DVN, MMR proof forgery, signed integer abuse in the insurance fund, and circular swap path exploitation in the margin trading protocol.

  • The KelpDAO incident ($290M) demonstrates that cross-chain bridge security has transcended the smart contract level, extending to off-chain verification infrastructure. The cascading freeze of WETH across five chains and the forced state transition of Arbitrum further reveal how composability amplifies single points of failure and where the actual trust boundaries of "decentralized" systems truly lie. In the past week (April 13, 2026 - April 19, 2026), BlockSec detected and analyzed four attack incidents, with a total estimated loss of approximately $310M. The table below summarizes these incidents, and subsequent sections will provide a detailed analysis of each incident.

    Table 1: Overview of the four attacks detected this week

    This week's highlight: KelpDAO

    This event was selected as this week's highlight because of its novel infrastructure-level attack vector (RPC poisoning against the unique DVN rather than smart contract exploitation), through DeFi The multi-chain cascading effects caused by composability, and the governance issues caused by Arbitrum's forced state transition to recover stolen funds. On April 18, 2026, KelpDAO's rsETH LayerZero OFT cross-chain bridge was attacked, resulting in a loss of approximately $290 million. LayerZero Labs attributed the attack to state-sponsored attackers, possibly the North Korean Lazarus Group [1]. The root cause was KelpDAO's 1-of-1 DVN configuration, which reduced cross-chain message verification to a single point of failure. The attackers poisoned the RPC infrastructure trusted by LayerZero Labs' DVN, forcing it to sign a forged cross-chain message, resulting in the release of 116,500 rsETH on Ethereum, while there was no corresponding sending event on the Unichain source chain. Background

    LayerZero is a cross-chain messaging protocol based on a modular security architecture. Its core cross-chain message integrity is guaranteed by a decentralized validator network (DVN). DVNs are off-chain entities responsible for independently verifying that a message sent on the source chain has indeed occurred before allowing execution on the target chain. Each application deployed on LayerZero configures its own DVN settings, including which DVNs to trust, how many DVNs to participate in, and the consensus threshold. This modularity gives applications complete control over the security model, but also means complete responsibility: weak configurations cannot be covered by the protocol itself. KelpDAO's rsETH is deployed as OFT (All-Chain Fungible Token) on LayerZero, bridging the Unichain (source chain) and the Ethereum mainnet (target chain). The OFT standard allows tokens to be destroyed on the source chain and released from lock on the target chain; cross-chain messages are the sole authorization for release. The Ethereum-side adapter (0x85d456...e98ef3) is responsible for releasing rsETH to the recipient after the cross-chain message is verified and delivered. The key issue is that KelpDAO configures this path as a 1-of-1 DVN setup, designating LayerZero Labs as the sole validator. This means that a single DVN proof can authorize the release of any token without second-party confirmation. To perform its verification duties, LayerZero Labs DVN queries multiple RPC nodes to confirm that cross-chain sending events did indeed occur on the source chain. These RPC nodes include self-operated infrastructure and external providers, and DVN relies on their collective responses to sign the proofs. The integrity of this process rests on the assumption that a majority of the queried nodes return genuine data. This vulnerability is a systemic failure at the infrastructure and configuration levels, consisting of three overlapping weaknesses. First, KelpDAO's 1-of-1 DVN configuration eliminates all redundancy at the verification layer. LayerZero's recommended security configuration explicitly requires multiple DVN setups and the use of independent validators to ensure that no single DVN can unilaterally authorize a message. By relying solely on LayerZero Labs DVNs, KelpDAO ensures that any breach of that single validator is sufficient to authorize arbitrary token releases. Secondly, DVN's failover mechanism routes validation queries to any reachable RPC node. This design assumes that node unavailability is incidental rather than intentional. However, this creates a condition where an attacker does not need to compromise all data sources: by taking healthy nodes offline via DDoS and having a poisoned node ready as the only reachable alternative, the attacker can have complete control over the data received by the DVN. Thirdly, replacing the op-geth executable on RPC nodes requires OS-level access to the underlying server. The exact initial access vector was not disclosed, but breaching two nodes on independent clusters likely indicates a common weakness in the access controls of these servers. These three conditions together form a complete attack chain: the first ensures no independent DVN can cross-validate the proven message; the second ensures the attacker has complete control over the data received by the single DVN; and the third provides the initial foothold that makes data manipulation possible. No single weakness is sufficient for success. Without a 1-of-1 configuration, the second DVN querying the independent infrastructure will reject the forged message. Without failover behavior, healthy nodes will veto the poisoned node. Without server compromise, the attacker will be unable to inject forged data. Attack Analysis The following analysis is based on transaction 0x1ae232...4222 and LayerZero Labs' official event statement. Step 1: The attacker obtained a list of specific RPC nodes trusted by LayerZero Labs DVN. This list constitutes a high-value intelligence target because knowing the exact nodes allows the attacker to plan precise surgical actions, rather than a broad infrastructure attack. Step 2: The attacker gained OS-level write access to two RPC nodes and replaced the running op-geth binary with a malicious version. These two nodes are described as running on independent, unconnected clusters, suggesting the initial access vector involved shared upstream dependencies (e.g., leaked deployment credentials, CI/CD pipelines, or social engineering attacks against operators with access to both). LayerZero Labs did not disclose the exact method of initial access. This step is a prerequisite for all subsequent data manipulation. Step 3: The malicious op-geth binary implements targeted response logic: it returns forged transaction data only to the DVN IP address, while providing the true blockchain state to all other requesters, including LayerZero's own monitoring infrastructure, block explorer, and scanning service. This selective poisoning makes the attack invisible to all existing observable systems; from any external perspective, the source chain appears to be functioning correctly. Step 4: DVN's internal consensus requires agreement between the poisoned and uncompromised RPC nodes. To resolve this conflict, the attacker launched a DDoS attack on the remaining healthy nodes during the attack window (10:20 to 11:40 PST), triggering DVN's failover logic and forcing it to rely entirely on the poisoned infrastructure. This step is essential because healthy nodes would otherwise return genuine data that contradicts the forged response. Step 5: Since DVN now only accepts data controlled by the attacker, a forged LayerZero cross-chain message is presented as valid. DVN proves nonce 308 on the Ethereum target endpoint, while this nonce has no corresponding outbound event on Unichain (the source endpoint still reports a maximum outbound nonce of 307). Step 6: After receiving the validly verified message, the Ethereum-side rsETH adapter releases 116,500 rsETH to the attacker's receiving address (0x8b1b6c...0d3b), which had been pre-funded via Tornado Cash several hours earlier. The stolen tokens are then distributed to seven branch wallets and liquidated through Aave staking, direct ETH exchange, and cross-chain settlement to Arbitrum. The final proceeds are aggregated to the Ethereum address 0x5d3919...7ccc and the corresponding aggregation address on Arbitrum. Step 7: After execution, the malicious binary triggers a self-destruct procedure, deleting itself and all local logs and configuration files. This greatly hindered the recovery of evidence after the incident, demonstrating the high level of operational sophistication of the attacker.

    Step 8:The attacker then attempted to attack again through the same path to obtain an additional 40,000 rsETH (approximately $95M), but was blocked after KelpDAO detected the anomaly and suspended the Ethereum mainnet and all related contracts on each L2 [2].

    Wider Impact

    The damage went far beyond the initial $290M cross-chain bridge vulnerability. The attacker deposited approximately 89,567 rsETH (approximately $221M) into Aave on multiple markets and lent out WETH with E-Mode 93% LTV [4]. Since Aave cannot distinguish between legitimately bridged rsETH and tokens released through forged messages on-chain, "toxic" collateral is considered fully valid. The resulting WETH reserve freeze spread from Ethereum to Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea, affecting users who had never touched rsETH. This cascading effect, from a single cross-chain bridge configuration defect to a multi-chain lending market disruption, illustrates how DeFi composability amplifies the scope and cost of a single point of failure. The subsequent developments also raised important questions about the realities of decentralized operation. LayerZero Labs announced that its DVN would no longer sign applications using 1-of-1 configurations [1], meaning that decentralization at the protocol level alone cannot compensate for configuration defects at the application level. At the chain level, the Arbitrum Security Council performed an emergency action, freezing 30,766 ETH held by the attacker on Arbitrum One. According to BlockSec analysis [5], this was achieved through a chain-level forced state transition: the Security Council temporarily upgraded the Ethereum inbox contract, injected an unsigned L1 to L2 message simulating the attacker's address, and then restored the original contract implementation, without requiring the holder's signature [3]. This operation was a legitimate exercise of emergency powers defined by the governance framework and was carried out transparently after coordination with law enforcement. However, it also shows that the L2 chain retains centralized emergency intervention capabilities in its design: in principle, any asset on Arbitrum One can be moved by the Security Council through the same mechanism. As this incident reveals at each layer, the gap between the theoretical trust model of the system and its actual trust boundary is the most critical risk. Conclusion This incident demonstrates that cross-chain bridge security cannot be reduced to mere protocol correctness. The LayerZero protocol itself operates as designed; the vulnerability resides entirely at the operational level above it. The core lesson is that off-chain verification infrastructure is part of the trust boundary, and its security posture must be commensurate with the value it protects. Any one of the following three mitigation measures can prevent this incident on its own: Multiple DVN Configurations: Requiring multiple independent DVNs to reach consensus will make a single compromised DVN insufficient to authorize a message, no matter how thoroughly the DVN is compromised. Fault-Aware RPC Selection: A sudden decrease in reachable nodes during an active verification window should be considered a potential attack signal rather than a regular availability event. DVN implementations should pause or alert when the node set decreases, rather than continuing operation solely on the remaining nodes. RPC Infrastructure Hardening: The ability to replace executables running on production RPC nodes indicates insufficient access control on the underlying servers. The infrastructure that DVN relies on to obtain the true state of the source chain should enjoy the same security boundaries as the DVN signature instance. More broadly, any cross-chain bridge or protocol that relies on off-chain proofs should be audited not only at the smart contract layer but also the entire data pipeline from source chain events to target chain execution. When hundreds of millions of dollars rely on RPC infrastructure, the assumption of trusting its reliability by default no longer holds.

    References

    [1] LayerZero Labs, "KelpDAO Incident Statement", April 20, 2026. https://x.com/LayerZero_Core/status/2046081551574983137

    [2] KelpDAO, "April 18 Incident: Additional Context", April 21, 2026. https://x.com/KelpDAO/status/2046332070277091807

    [3] Arbitrum, "Security Council Emergency Action," April 21, 2026. https://x.com/arbitrum/status/2046435443680346189

    [4] LlamaRisk, "rsETH Incident Report", April 20, 2026. https://governance.aave.com/t/rseth-incident-report-april-20-2026/24580

    [5] BlockSec, "Arbitrum Security Council Freeze Mechanism Analysis," April 21, 2026. https://x.com/Phalcon_xyz/status/2046467830498173088

    Other Events This Week

    Hyperbridge

    On April 13, 2026, Hyperbridge, an Ethereum-based cross-chain messaging bridge, was attacked due to a missing input verification in its MMR (Merkle Mountain Range) proof verification logic, resulting in a loss of approximately $242,000. The function MerkleMountainRange.VerifyProof() failed to enforce a check that leaf_index < leafCount, allowing attackers to forge cross-chain proofs and perform privileged operations, including minting 1,000,000,000 DOT tokens.

    However, VerifyProof() only checks if root == CalculateRoot(proof, leaves, mmrSize) is true, and does not verify whether each leaf.index is within the valid range (i.e., leaf.index < leafCount). By choosing leafCount = 1 and leaf_index = 1, an attacker makes CalculateRoot() skip the process of folding the forged request commitment into the calculated root, directly returning the peak root. This breaks the message-to-proof binding, allowing arbitrary payloads to pass verification in the name of the historical overlayRoot.

    MerkleMountainRange.VerifyProof(root, request.proof.multiproof, leaves, request.proof.leafCount) ...>

    Figure: Code snippet of missing leaf_index boundary check in VerifyProof function

    Attack Analysis

    The following analysis is based on transaction 0x240aeb...1109 [1]. Step 1: Attacker EOA 0xC513...F8E7 deployed auxiliary contracts 0x518A...8f26 and 0x31a1...ca9AB in the same transaction. Step 2: Auxiliary contract 0x31a1...ca9AB submitted a forged request through the vulnerability verification path of HandlerV1. Because VerifyProof() did not reject an out-of-bounds leaf_index, the forged request commitment was excluded from root computation, but the proof still matched the historical overlayRoot. Step 3: After the forged message is accepted, HandlerV1 distributes it to TokenGateway, executing the ChangeAssetAdmin operation. This changes the administrator of the DOT tokens to an attacker-controlled auxiliary contract 0x31a1...ca9AB. Step 4: The auxiliary contract mints 1,000,000,000e18 DOT tokens. Step 5: The auxiliary contract exchanges the newly minted DOT tokens for 108.2 ETH via Odos Router V3. Step 6: The attacker transferred 108.2 ETH to their EOA account. Conclusion This incident was caused by improper proof verification in Hyperbridge's MMR verification logic. Because the check `leaf_index < leafCount` was not enforced, the attacker could forge a message whose promise was never actually included in the computed root, yet still pass the verification of the historical state root. Mitigation measures should enforce strict boundary checks, such as `leaf_index < leafCount`, before proof verification. References [1] BlockSec, "Hyperbridge Attack Analysis", April 13, 2026. https://x.com/Phalcon_xyz/status/2043601549893738970 On April 13, 2026, Dango, a perpetual contract DEX built on Cosmos AppChain, was attacked due to a lack of symbol checks, resulting in a loss of approximately $1.5 million. The function `replenish_insurance_fund()` uses `is_non_zero()` instead of `is_positive()` to validate the input amount, allowing an attacker to provide a negative `UsdValue` and transfer the insurance fund to their own margin position. Dango is a perpetual contract DEX built on the Cosmos AppChain. Users deposit USDC as collateral into the perpetual contract and open leveraged long or short positions in assets such as BTC, ETH, and SOL via the on-chain Central Limit Order Book (CLOB). Each user's collateral balance is tracked within the perpetual contract as a margin account. To protect liquidity providers (LPs) from bad debt losses, the protocol maintains an insurance fund: USDC reserves held within perpetual contracts to cover the shortfall when the collateral for a liquidated position is insufficient to fully repay the debt. Without the insurance fund, these shortfalls would be directly distributed to the LPs. Any user can contribute to the insurance fund from their perpetual account margin. The root cause lies in the function `replenish_insurance_fund()` in contract 0x90bc84...bea4f, which failed to reject negative input. The function has two checks, but neither can intercept negative amounts: `ensure!(amount.is_non_zero())` checks if the amount is non-zero, but does not check if it is positive. `ensure!(user_state.margin >= amount)` checks if the user has sufficient margin, but any positive margin satisfies `>= negative`. After both checks pass, the function executes `user_state.margin.checked_sub_assign(amount)` and `state.insurance_fund.checked_add_assign(amount)`. When the amount is negative, subtracting the negative number increases the user's deposit, while adding the negative number decreases the insurance fund, completely reversing the expected flow of funds.

    Figure: Missing symbol check for the replenish_insurance_fund function

    Attack analysis

    Transactions:

    Figure: Transaction trace of attacker depositing USDC to open a margin position

    Step 2:The attacker called replenish_insurance_fund() with a negative amount (i.e., -1,500,000). Due to improper validation, the negative amount was accepted, and the insurance fund's assets were transferred to the attacker's margin position.

    Step 3: The attacker withdraws all assets from the margin position, obtaining $1,500,000 USDC.

    Figure: Transaction trace of attacker withdrawing stolen funds from margin position

    Phase 2:

    In Tx 2-8, the attacker calls transfer_remote() to bridge the stolen assets to Ethereum.

    Ultimately, $410,000 of USDC was bridged to Ethereum. The essence of this attack lies in the lack of sign protection when using a signed integer type in an unsigned context. The UsdValue type is designed to be signed (the perpetual contract PnL can be negative), but the insurance fund donation path only makes sense for positive contributions. Using is_non_zero() instead of is_positive() leaves a one-word gap, allowing any caller to reverse the flow of funds, transferring USDC from the insurance fund to their own margin. The attacker completed the entire attack in a single transaction (deposit $1, transfer $1.5M, withdraw $1,500,001), then slowly bridged the funds. The bridging rate limit is the only mechanism that limits the loss: without it, the entire approximately $1.5M would have been irreversibly bridged to Ethereum. On April 16, 2026, Burrowland, the lending and margin trading protocol on Rhea Finance's NEAR platform, was attacked due to a business logic flaw in its margin trading module, resulting in a loss of approximately $18.4 million. When opening a leveraged position, the protocol relies on `verify_token_out()` to verify the expected swap output before accepting the position. However, this function incorrectly incremented the `token_out` amount when the output tokens in intermediate swap steps matched the final output tokens, failing to account for these intermediate amounts being subsequently reused as `token_in`. The attacker deployed fake tokens and pools, then constructed a circular swap path, inflating the perceived output amount and passing solvency checks, thus extracting approximately $18.4 million from the protocol. Background Burrowland is an open-source lending and margin trading protocol on NEAR. In addition to standard deposit and borrowing functionality, it supports margin trading and introduces three key variables to represent a user's leveraged position: token_c (collateral), token_d (liability asset), and token_p (position asset). For a long position, the user deposits token_c as collateral and borrows token_d with a selected leverage (e.g., 5x). The borrowed token_d is then exchanged for token_p on the DEX, representing the asset the user wishes to expose. Normally, the value of the received token_p is approximately equal to the value of the spent token_d. The protocol holds token_p on behalf of the user, while recording borrowed token_d as debt. For short positions, the user similarly deposits token_c and borrows token_d (the asset they wish to short) using leverage. The borrowed token_d is exchanged for another asset (token_p), effectively establishing a short exposure to token_d. Again, under normal market conditions, the exchange is expected to maintain its value. Throughout the position's lifecycle, token_p is held in escrow by the protocol and cannot be directly withdrawn by the user. The position must be closed to realize profit or loss, at which point token_p is exchanged back to token_d to repay the debt. Opening a margin position is handled by `internal_margin_open_position()`, which sets the position parameters and distributes the borrowing to the DEX.

    Figure: internal_margin_open_position function settings

    Before the protocol accepts a new position, it evaluates four protections in sequence: is_min_amount_out_reasonable() cross-checks the user-declared min_token_p_amount with the implicit swap output of the Pyth oracle to limit slippage, is_open_position_liquidatable() The `is_open_position_forcecloseable()` function verifies whether the expected position and collateral value exceed the liquidation threshold, `get_open_position_lr()` verifies whether the account is not insolvent on paper, and `token_d/token_c` forces the value to not exceed the maximum leverage ratio. All four checks use `min_token_p_amount` as the value of the position assets because the exchange has not yet been executed and no actual amount is available. Therefore, the correctness of each checkpoint depends on the binding relationship between `min_token_p_amount` and the actual amount delivered by the DEX. This binding is precisely what `verify_token_out()` (implemented via `RefV1TokenReceiverMessage::get_token_out()`) should perform on the user-submitted exchange message.

    Figure: verify_token_out and solvency check

    Vulnerability Analysis

    The flaw is inside verify_token_out().

    This function selects the token_out from the last swap step as the final output token, and then sums the declared min_amount_out of each swap step that produces the same token, assuming that each such output contributes to the final output. This is correct for true multi-path (split-route) swaps, but it does not exclude steps where token_out is immediately consumed as token_in in the next step. Round-trip paths such as A->B->A->B will cause each ->B step to be included in the sum, even if its output is consumed in the subsequent B->A step and never reaches Burrowland. The accumulated min_amount_out approved by verify_token_out() no longer represents the amount actually returned by the DEX.

    Figure: verify_token_out function

    Fake token ID:

    • Fake1：31623e1d98275d2b0db4f50e102f6bf40877c1345e06e4ca6727f58c89564bb2

    • Fake2：6a28e3d3c7af1415ec22c6264013e1138bab00f85b8b6055d882d7d46afdf49b

    • Fake3: e081e03daf58f5bb04cf95a03017e58449b76e704f1974771d7e3bd52835b6e5

    Fake pool ID:

    • Zec-Fake1：7509

    • Fake1-Fake2：7510

    • USDC-Fake2：7511

    • Fake3-USDC: 7513

    Phase 2: Opening a Margin Position

    Step 1:Using Burrowland's margin trading feature, the attacker opened a leveraged position using a legitimate asset as token_c and a real reserve asset as token_d, along with an exchange message. The operation list is a round trip A->B->A->B, entirely routed through the pool controlled by the attacker in Phase 1.

  • Fake3-USDC: 7513

Phase 2: Opening a Margin Position

Step 1:Using Burrowland's margin trading feature, the attacker opened a leveraged position using a legitimate asset as token_c and a real reserve asset as token_d, along with an exchange message. The operation list is a round trip A->B->A->B, entirely routed through the pool controlled by the attacker in Phase 1.

Figure: Transaction trace of an attacker opening leveraged positions using a round-trip path

Step 2:Because verify_token_out() accumulates the min_amount_out of all steps where token_out matches the final output, the round-trip path allows the attacker to inflate the declared min_token_p_amount to any value.

Step 2:Because verify_token_out() accumulates the min_amount_out of all steps where token_out matches the final output, the round-trip path allows the attacker to inflate the declared min_token_p_amount to any value.

Conclusion

This incident was caused by a business logic flaw in the Burrowland margin opening path. The function RefV1TokenReceiverMessage::get_token_out() incorrectly accumulated intermediate outputs when matching them with the final tokens, assuming these amounts would be retained as the final output. However, the circular swap path breaks this assumption because these tokens can be reused and consumed within the path. Therefore, the calculated min_token_p_amount can be artificially inflated, causing all subsequent solvency checks to rely on an incorrect value, resulting in positions being opened in a falsely healthy state without verifying the actual amount received.

Conclusion

This incident was caused by a business logic flaw in the Burrowland margin opening path. The function RefV1TokenReceiverMessage::get_token_out() incorrectly accumulated intermediate outputs when matching them with the final tokens, assuming these amounts would be retained as the final output.
