data.title: Strategy's perpetual motion machine for buying coins stalled after the price fell below $100.
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

The $100 anchor was once the cornerstone of Strategy's fundraising magic. This perpetual motion machine of fundraising, built by Michael Saylor to buy Bitcoin, is now stuck.

On April 14, Strategy's perpetual preferred stock STRC fell below the $100 par value anchor on Nasdaq, hitting a low of $99.06, with trading volume plummeting to 47% of normal, and has continued to trade at a discount since then ...p>

STRC 11.5% High Interest Rate Locked in Price, Strategy Creates a Perpetual Motion Machine for Buying Crypto

In July 2025, STRC was officially launched, solving Saylor's pain point:Without diluting the voting rights of MSTR common stock, it continuously draws funds from the traditional capital market to buy Bitcoin. The original design intent of STRC was to keep the trading price around $100, ensuring the company could continuously raise funds through an "at-the-market" (ATM) process. If the price remains below $100, the board will raise the dividend to attract investors seeking stable cash flow to support the price; if the price is significantly above $100, the dividend will be maintained or lowered to reduce financing costs. Starting with an initial 9% annualized dividend, STRC has raised its dividend for seven consecutive months, currently reaching 11.5%. A steady stream of investors entered the market seeking stable, high returns, keeping the STRC consistently above its face value. This allowed Saylor to convert money from the traditional market into buying power in the Bitcoin market through its ATM system. Furthermore, Saylor abandoned the traditional capital market's net profit valuation model, instead adopting the "Bitcoin Gain" metric to define Strategy's value as a "Bitcoin-standard" company. This metric measures the percentage increase in Bitcoin holdings per share of common stock. In the first quarter of 2026, Strategy achieved a 6.2% Bitcoin gain, with a full-year target of 9.5%. STRC is the leverage tool for achieving this goal: It involves purchasing Bitcoin with long-term appreciation potential by issuing preferred stock with fixed financing costs. According to Saylor's calculations, as long as Bitcoin's annualized growth rate exceeds 2.05% in the long term, ordinary shareholders will continue to benefit. Over the past six months, this logic has repeated itself: raising funds through STRC → buying Bitcoin → Bitcoin price increases → stock market capitalization increases → STRC becomes more sought after → raising more money to buy more Bitcoin. STRC is like a never-ending money-printing machine, continuously supplying Saylor's Bitcoin empire with ammunition. STRC falls below the $100 anchor point; Strategy unveils bi-weekly dividend payout as a surprise measure. The $100 face value is the lifeblood of STRC's entire financing flywheel. Once it falls below this level, ATM issuance will come to a standstill, and the printing press will stop running. This decoupling is a double blow from macroeconomic headwinds and deteriorating expectations. The Iran war became the first straw that broke the back of STRC. The disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and the surge in oil prices have fueled inflation concerns, pushing market expectations for a Federal Reserve interest rate cut from mid-2026 to 2027. For preferred stocks like STRC, which have strong bond-like characteristics, the persistently high benchmark interest rate means that its 11.5% dividend attractiveness is being diluted by rising risk-free rates. Meanwhile, the crypto market's Fear & Greed Index once plummeted to 9, indicating a state of "extreme fear." Funds that were originally chasing stable returns began selling off non-core assets, with the relatively illiquid STRC bearing the brunt. If the macroeconomic environment was the external trigger, then the dividend decision on April 1st was the needle that pricked the bubble. On this day, Strategy announced it would maintain its 11.5% dividend, but ended its seven-month streak of monthly interest rate hikes. PANews believes the company's intention was to convey confidence to the market: interest rates have reached a steady state and prices are close to parity. However, investors misinterpreted this move as: the company's fundraising capabilities have peaked, and it has lost confidence in Bitcoin's subsequent price increases. Retail investors account for as much as 80% of STRC holders. Their entry was driven by the ingrained expectation of "monthly interest rate hikes and prices stabilizing above par value." However, the initial halt in price increases shattered this belief, leading to a mass exodus of retail investors, a sharp drop in trading volume, and the collapse of the par value anchor. The drop of STRC below par value not only affected Strategy itself but also the entire supply and demand dynamics of the Bitcoin market. When the price of STRC falls below its face value, issuing more ATMs at market price becomes meaningless. Issuing at a discount will further depress the price, creating a vicious cycle that will force Strategy to stop issuing more ATMs. Real-world data also confirms this: the latest funding tracker shows zero new funding for STRC. Strategy's large purchase of 34,164 Bitcoins last week used the remaining funds from the previous funding round. With the suspension of STRC's funding, this largest bullish force in the Bitcoin market has temporarily halted. The Bitcoin market has also lost the marginal buying support of $1-2 billion per week. Faced with the crisis of its tools failing, Strategy quickly intervened, attempting to regain pricing power through financial means. Strategy announced that it will launch a shareholder vote on April 28th, proposing to increase STRC's dividend frequency from once a month to once every two weeks. This is a precise psychological tactic targeting retail investors. By shortening the dividend payment cycle, it reduces the price gap caused by the ex-dividend date (the trading day following the record date for dividend distribution). Historically, STRC has fallen by an average of 45 cents on the ex-dividend date and taken about 12 days to return to par value. The bi-weekly cash flow return significantly reduces reinvestment lag for investors, making it more attractive to cash flow-sensitive retail investors and income funds. Once the proposal is passed, STRC will become one of the very few listed equity instruments globally to offer bi-weekly dividend payments. To counter market accusations of Ponzi scheme tactics, Strategy also emphasized the depth of its non-Bitcoin assets. According to disclosures, the company currently holds approximately $2.25 billion in cash reserves, sufficient to cover approximately 30 months of dividend obligations for all preferred shares without issuing new shares or selling Bitcoin. Furthermore, its traditional business intelligence software business generates $320 million in gross profit annually, ensuring the company's survival even in extreme market conditions. Despite having Bitcoin reserves as backing, controversy surrounding STRC has never ceased. Traditional financial experts like Peter Schiff believe that Bitcoin itself does not generate any returns, and STRC's high dividends are actually achieved by attracting new investors or sacrificing the interests of ordinary shareholders. Their logic is: Bitcoin price falls → STRC price falls → financing function is lost → unable to continue buying Bitcoin to support the price → forced to sell Bitcoin to pay dividends → Bitcoin price falls further. While Strategy can prevent a "death spiral" through proactive intervention, it will then face a dilemma: either significantly dilute common stockholders' equity to raise funds, or continue to increase yields to maintain attractiveness, paying higher financing costs. Therefore, STRC is unlikely to experience a UST-like death spiral, but it does face a "self-reinforcing" downside risk, more akin to "chronic blood loss." Strategy countered that the STRC model is based on Bitcoin's appreciation potential as a long-term deflationary asset. As long as Bitcoin's appreciation exceeds the financing cost, preferred stock financing will generate a positive asset accretion effect, rather than a Ponzi scheme of transferring funds from one source to another. According to their disclosure, Bitcoin reserves currently cover preferred stock principal by more than 4.3 times, meaning that STRC would only face substantial insolvency if Bitcoin fell below approximately $18,000. However, the capital market always reacts before fundamentals. Before reaching this threshold, the secondary market price of STRC may collapse due to panic in the Bitcoin market. It is worth noting that investors often overlook its underlying legal definition when participating in STRC trading. While it nominally has a face value and fixed dividends, legally it belongs to the equity security category, lacking the mandatory principal repayment obligation of bonds and having no fixed maturity date. In the order of capital redemption, STRC ranks after convertible bonds, secured bonds, and other debt categories. The drop of STRC below $100 is a significant hurdle that Bitcoin, as a DAT asset, must overcome to reach maturity. For ordinary investors, STRC's de-pegging serves as a wake-up call. In the crypto market, no form of "pegging" is absolute; liquidity is always the primary principle for survival. While STRC's current high yield of 11.5% is tempting, it conceals credit risk and a liquidity trap. In the journey towards a "Bitcoin standard," the fact that STRC has fallen below its par value is merely a brief interlude. Ensuring the structural stability of the financing machine while pursuing greater scale is the key to winning this long-term race.

data.title: US soldier charged with insider trading; Polymarket's gray area coming to an end?
data.description:
Written by: Shannon@Golden Finance

The widely publicized case of leaking insider information about Maduro's arrest has finally come to a close.

On April 23, 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice released an indictment in the Manhattan Federal Court in New York, arresting a U.S. soldier who knew in advance about Maduro's arrest and used this information to place bets on Polymarket. He is a 38-year-old active-duty U.S. Army soldier named Gannon Ken Van Dyke.

This marks the first time that the long-standing legal gray area of ​​"prediction market insider trading" has become a concrete criminal prosecution. This U.S. soldier will be permanently recorded in the U.S. federal judicial archives as the defendant in the first case of prediction market insider trading.

I. The Core of the Case: A Military Operation, a Prediction Market, and a Soldier's Greed

Van Dyke is an active-duty U.S. Army soldier stationed at Fort Bragg, Fayetteville, North Carolina.

Due to the nature of his work, he signed a confidentiality agreement, promising "never to disclose any classified or sensitive information in writing, verbally, physically, or otherwise." However, starting December 8, 2025, he began participating in the planning and execution of a military operation codenamed "Operation Absolute Resolve"—a secret U.S. mission to capture Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela. During this time, he obtained sensitive classified information about the operation. What did he do with this information? He placed bets on Polymarket. II. Detailed Transaction Timeline: 13 bets, $33,034 principal, $400,000 profit. December 8, 2025 Van Dyke began participating in the planning and execution of "Operation Absolute Resolve," obtaining classified information about the military operation. On December 14, 2025, Van Dyke created an email address unrelated to his real name in preparation for concealing his identity. On December 26, 2025, Van Dyke created an account on Polymarket, deposited funds, and began trading in markets related to Maduro and Venezuela. From December 27, 2025 to January 26, 2026 (betting phase), Van Dyke made approximately 13 trades, all betting on the "YES" direction, covering the following contracts: "US troops enter Venezuela before January 31"; "Maduro steps down before January 31"; "US invades Venezuela before January 31"; "Trump uses war powers against Venezuela before January 31". He invested approximately $33,034 while possessing classified information.

Early morning of January 3, 2026 (the day of the operation)

US forces raided Maduro's residence in Caracas before dawn, arresting him and his wife. Hours later, the US President publicly announced the success of the operation. Immediately afterwards, Polymarket settled multiple related contracts as "YES".

On the same day as the operation, Van Dyke withdrew most of his illicit gains from his Polymarket account and transferred most of the funds to an offshore cryptocurrency vault, which was then deposited into a newly created online brokerage account.

Total profits: $409,881.

January 6, 2026 (cover-up phase)

After the operation became public, reports of abnormal trading in Maduro-related contracts on Polymarket began to appear in the media and on social media.

Van Dyke then proceeded to conceal his identity: claiming he couldn't access his registered email address, he requested Polymarket to delete his account; on the same day, he changed the registration email for his cryptocurrency exchange account to an email address he had created earlier on December 14th, registered under a name other than his real name. The Department of Justice released the indictment on April 23, 2026 (the date of indictment). Van Dyke is charged with three counts of violating the Commodity Exchange Act, one count of wire fraud, and one count of illegal currency trading, which carries a maximum sentence of 50 years in prison. He appeared in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina, and the case was subsequently transferred to Judge Margaret M. Garnett for the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. In Van Dyke's case, the indictment currently lists only one defendant: Van Dyke himself. The indictment makes no mention of accomplices or associates. Van Dyke created the account, placed bets, transferred funds, and carried out the concealment alone. However, there is no official disclosure yet as to whether investigators are still pursuing other insiders. III. Summary of a Series of Manipulation and Insider Trading Cases on Polymarket The Van Dyke case is not an isolated incident; it is the first case in a series of insider trading scandals on Polymarket in the past year to result in criminal prosecution. The following is a complete list of cases that are documented to date: Case 1: Van Dyke Case (United States, December 2025 - January 2026) – **Indicted** A U.S. Army soldier used classified information from "Operation Absolute Resolve" to bet approximately $33,034 on Polymarket, profiting approximately $409,881. He was ultimately federally indicted on April 23, 2026. Case Two: Israeli Air Force's Iran Strike Insider Case (June 2025 to Early 2026) – **Indicted, 2 Arrested** In June 2025, an Israeli Air Force reserve major attended a classified briefing on Operation Rising Lion (Israel's 12-Day War against Iran) and learned the specific date of the first strike. He sent a message via WhatsApp to his civilian friend, informing him that the attack would take place on the night of June 12th and advising him to "significantly increase his bets." The civilian opened an account on Polymarket and placed bets, successfully predicting four security events: Israel would attack Iran on Friday; Israel would attack Iran before the end of June 2025, etc. According to Israeli media reports, the two men profited approximately $162,663 from their first successful bet, which they split equally. In September 2025, the major again shared information about Israel's strike on Yemen with his friend, winning him approximately $5,000. The two sides continued their cooperation, attempting to bet on the timing of the next Iranian strike, but abandoned the effort after posts about their accounts appeared on social media, and subsequently deleted all WhatsApp communications between them. The investigation revealed that at least three other Israeli Air Force members were questioned for allegedly using classified information about Iranian strikes to place bets on Polymarket. The lead investigator accused the Israeli Air Force of having a "misorganized culture regarding classified information." One crew member questioned admitted during questioning: "The entire squadron was using Polymarket, the entire Air Force was betting." Two key suspects (a reserve major and a civilian) were charged with "serious security offenses," bribery, and obstruction of justice, and were ordered to remain in custody until the proceedings conclude. Case 3: Google's "Search of the Year" Ranking Insider Case (December 2025) – **No Prosecution, Identity Unidentified** In late 2025, a Polymarket trader using the pseudonym "AlphaRaccoon" placed a series of precise bets around the time of Google's 2025 "Search of the Year" rankings release, profiting approximately $1 million within 24 hours. The account also accurately predicted the release date of a new Google product, earning over $150,000. With 22 out of 23 trades resulting in profit, statistically, it's almost impossible to attribute this to luck, leading observers to consider it clear insider trading. The community widely suspects the bettor was a Google employee. No one has been arrested or prosecuted yet. Case 4: White House Insider Suspicions Regarding Iran Strikes and Tariff Policy (April 2025 to March 2026) – Under Investigation In February 2026, six accounts on Polymarket bet that "the US will strike Iran by February 28, 2026," and the event subsequently occurred. On March 23, within minutes of Trump's post threatening to "destroy" Iranian power plants, the volume of futures contracts betting on a drop in oil prices surged several times over, and oil prices subsequently plummeted after Trump announced "very good talks" with Iran. Analysts called the trading "clearly unusual." The BBC investigation also revealed similar unusual trading behavior dating back to April 2025, just three months after the start of Trump's second term, when Trump announced "Liberation Day" tariffs followed by a 90-day suspension; similarly unusual advance betting in related financial markets occurred. Currently, the identities of those behind these transactions have not been publicly confirmed, and the investigation is ongoing. Case 5: OpenAI Browser Insider Betting (October 2025) – **Unidentified** In October 2025, a new Polymarket user bet $40,000, predicting that OpenAI would launch an AI web browser by the end of the month, ultimately winning nearly 20%. The accuracy of the bet and the timing of the account creation raised widespread questions about insider information, but no one has been prosecuted so far. Case 6: French Weather Sensor Hair Dryer Manipulation Case (Early to Mid-April 2026) – **Confirmed Market Manipulation** Polymarket had a weather forecast contract that relied on readings from the only temperature sensor at Charles de Gaulle Airport. A "malicious genius" went to the airport with a hair dryer just before the contract closed, blowing air onto the sensor to artificially inflate the temperature reading, turning an extreme temperature bet with only a 1% chance of winning into reality, successfully manipulating the contract outcome. France subsequently launched an investigation into the case. See Jinse Finance's previous report: Polymarket's Ridiculous Manipulation Reappears: Hair Dryer "Blows" Out $34,000

Case Seven: Third Israeli Air Force Member Bets on Yemen and Subsequent Iran Contracts (2025-2026) - Under Investigation

According to Polymarket's Wikipedia page, in addition to the above main cases, another Israeli Air Force crew member was questioned separately for betting on the Iran-Iraq War on the 12th, reportedly profiting approximately $46,000.

Case 8: Kalshi Congressional Candidates Bet on Their Own Election (2026) – **Punished** Kalshi imposed fines and a five-year platform ban on three congressional candidates in 2026 because they bet on themselves to run for office after contracts related to "whether someone will run" opened, subsequently publicly announcing their candidacy and completing a full loop of insider arbitrage. IV. Conclusion: A Formal Crackdown on Insider Trading in Prediction Markets? Polymarket CEO Shayne Coplan, in an interview with CBS News, defended insider trading, stating that insiders "have an advantage in the market," and that platforms "provide economic incentives for people to disclose information to the market." While this statement has academic basis in terms of information efficiency, it has caused a huge uproar on both moral and legal levels. U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton of the Manhattan District Attorney's Office had previously issued a clear warning at a securities law conference: "Being involved in a prediction market does not exempt you from fraud charges." Subsequently, the Manhattan District Attorney's Office's Securities and Commodity Fraud Division met with representatives of Polymarket to discuss how current laws apply to potential misconduct in the industry. Harvard University researchers estimate that, in the documented series of cases, insiders may have profited approximately $143 million by placing bets in advance on Polymarket. The Van Dyke case illustrates two points: first, the application of federal law is not exempt from the novelty of "prediction markets"; second, on Polymarket's encrypted and anonymous platform, insider traders who believed that blockchain wallets could provide permanent protection ultimately underestimated the FBI's ability to trace insiders. As Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche stated, "Wide access to prediction markets is a relatively new phenomenon, but federal laws protecting national security information are fully applicable." The historical significance of this case lies not in the $400,000 profit, but in the fact that it marks the first formal crackdown by the U.S. judicial system on the legal gray area of ​​"prediction market insider trading." Following this, anyone possessing classified government information and eyeing Polymarket contracts should recalculate their risk-reward ratio.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Launches Stablecoin Reserve Fund — A Major Wall Street Signal You Can’t Ignore
data.description:
Built for a Post-Regulation Stablecoin Era

Morgan Stanley has launched a dedicated stablecoin reserve portfolio, signaling a deeper push by traditional finance into the rapidly evolving digital asset ecosystem.

The new product, known as the Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio (MSNXX), is structured within the bank’s Institutional Liquidity Funds platform and allows stablecoin issuers to hold backing assets in a regulated money market framework while earning yield.

MORGAN STANLEY LAUNCHES STABLECOIN RESERVES FUND

Morgan Stanley Investment Management has launched the Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio (MSNXX). It is a government money market fund built exclusively for stablecoin issuers.

The fund aligns with reserve requirements set out under… pic.twitter.com/ynDaPGPr8y

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 24, 2026

Designed to maintain a stable $1 net asset value, the fund invests in cash, short-term U.S. Treasuries with maturities of 93 days or less, and Treasury-backed repurchase agreements. It offers daily liquidity and income distribution, with a minimum investment of $10 million and a 0.15% management fee.

The launch comes as the U.S. sharpens its regulatory stance on stablecoins under the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins Act, commonly known as the GENIUS Act. The legislation emphasizes high-quality, liquid reserves—requirements that align closely with traditional money market fund structures.

💥BREAKING:

US just introduced official stablecoin rules under the GENIUS Act.

1:1 reserves, instant redemption, no rehypothecation.

This is the foundation for institutional adoption. pic.twitter.com/m73oxevGnd

— Crypto Rover (@cryptorover) April 8, 2026

Morgan Stanley framed the new portfolio as a response to this shift, positioning it as a compliant solution for issuers navigating stricter oversight while maintaining capital stability and liquidity. Amy Oldenburd, the firm's head of digital asset strategy explained

“Developing innovative ways to work with stablecoin issuers is another step towards modernizing the financial infrastructure.”

The move also reflects growing interest from both crypto-native firms and traditional payment providers, as stablecoins gain traction for cross-border payments and treasury management.

While tailored for stablecoin issuers, the fund is also open to other institutional investors, underscoring its broader role as a bridge between traditional finance and blockchain-based liabilities.

MORGAN STANLEY JUST POINTED 16,000 ADVISORS AT BITCOIN. 🚨

THIS WEEK.
Every client. Every portfolio. Every conversation.
Bitcoin.

Not a crypto exchange.
Not a retail app.
The biggest bank on Wall Street.

Selling Bitcoin ETFs to the richest people on earth.
16,000 advisors.… pic.twitter.com/rDda944kcH

— Crypto Tice (@CryptoTice_) April 10, 2026

The firm expects the fund’s shares to be held primarily by stablecoin issuers seeking compliant reserve solutions, although access is not strictly limited and may extend to other qualified investors.

As one of the world’s largest investment banks, Morgan Stanley brings significant scale to the initiative, with approximately 16,000 financial advisers overseeing more than $6 trillion in client assets—underscoring the weight of its entry into the stablecoin infrastructure space.

The structure mirrors conventional money market funds but adapts them to support tokenized dollar systems—an increasingly important layer of financial infrastructure as stablecoin adoption expands.

Part of a Broader Crypto Expansion

Morgan Stanley’s is one of the several Wall Street firms that have pushed aggresively into the crypto market recently to meet rising instituitonal client demand. Earlier this month, the firm launched a Bitcoin exchange-traded fund that quickly surpassed $100 million in inflows, highlighting strong early demand despite competition from larger players such as BlackRock and Fidelity.

MORGAN STANLEY BUYS $17,000,000+ IN BITCOIN$MSBT bought 215+ BTC today worth $17M, making it’s 8th consecutive day of inflows since launch (April 8).

Will they continue the Buy streak? pic.twitter.com/X1LxKS3vev

— Bitcoin Archive (@BitcoinArchive) April 17, 2026

Beyond ETFs, the bank has signaled ambitions to deepen its crypto capabilities further. Regulatory filings indicate plans to pursue a national trust banking charter, which would enable it to offer custody services and execute crypto transactions directly. Additional proposals for Ether and staked Solana ETFs point to a widening product pipeline.

Morgan Stanley’s entry into the stablecoin reserve market places it among a growing group of financial institutions seeking to capture a critical piece of the digital asset stack: the underlying reserves that support dollar-pegged tokens.

📣 JUST IN! Beth Haddock, Board Member and Global Policy Lead at Stablecoin Standard, says stablecoins are here to stay and will become foundational to the future of finance as adoption grows; noting banks are already "engaging" through custody and other outsourced services. pic.twitter.com/R2v9yJ5BI8

— 🇬🇧 ChartNerd 📊 (@ChartNerdTA) April 23, 2026

As regulatory clarity improves and institutional demand builds, control over these reserve assets is emerging as a key battleground—one that could reshape how traditional finance integrates with blockchain-based systems.

data.title: OpenAI to the left, DeepSeek to the right
data.description:
On April 24, 2026, DeepSeek V4 preview was officially released. This domestically developed large-scale model, including a Pro version with 1.6 trillion parameters and a Flash version with 284 billion parameters, focused its core selling point on the market: millions of contexts became a free standard feature in all official services. Around the same time, OpenAI across the ocean also released GPT-5.5. It boasted even greater computing power and richer agent functionality, but was also significantly more expensive. In layman's terms, "millions of contexts" means that AI is no longer a "goldfish" that can only remember the first few sentences you say, but has become a "super brain" that can devour three volumes of "The Three-Body Problem" in one go, understand a two-hour movie in a second, and even pick out your typos. For a direct example, you can dump all your company's contracts, emails, and financial reports from the past three years onto V4 and let it find the breach of contract clause hidden in the attachment on page 47. In the past, this would have required a legal team; now, it's free. GPT-5.5 sets a price tag for this super brain: the standard version costs $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens; while the GPT-5.5 Pro version, designed for advanced tasks, sells for a staggering $30 per million input tokens and $180 per million output tokens. However, according to DeepSeek's official pricing, V4-Flash cache-hit inputs cost only 0.2 RMB per million tokens and outputs cost 2 RMB; even V4-Pro, comparable to top-tier closed-source models, costs 1 RMB for cache-hit inputs, 12 RMB for cache-miss inputs, and only 24 RMB for outputs. People often think the US-China AI competition is a race of model capabilities, but in reality, it has long since become a divergence in business models. OpenAI, once the dragon-slaying hero proclaiming its mission to "benefit all mankind," is now selling expensive, fully-furnished apartments; while DeepSeek is turning AI into utility like water, electricity, and gas, using almost free computing power. When OpenAI becomes a shrewd contractor, why is DeepSeek turning top-tier AI into free tap water, regardless of cost? What undercurrents are hidden behind this shift in pricing power? The decisive match of the large-scale model took place in a server room in Inner Mongolia at -20 degrees Celsius. Shortly before the V4 release, DeepSeek added an unexpected position to its job postings: Senior Data Center Delivery Manager and Senior Operations Engineer, with a maximum monthly salary of 30,000 RMB, 14 months' pay, and on-site assignment in Ulanqab, Inner Mongolia. This is a company that once touted itself as an asset-light company focused on "minimalism, purity, and solely algorithm development." Over the past two years, their proudest achievement has been "achieving great results with minimal effort," using less than $6 million in training costs to release DeepSeek-R1, which caused a sharp drop in the US AI stock market. However, the massive computing power requirements of V4, coupled with increasingly stringent US restrictions on computing power, completely shattered this idyllic, asset-light model. In 2025, the US Department of Commerce further tightened export controls on AI chips to China, cutting off supplies of Nvidia's H100 and H800 chips, and even adding the downgraded H20 to the control list. This means that DeepSeek's future computing power expansion must fully shift to the Huawei Ascend ecosystem. In the V4 release statement, the official statement explicitly stated that the new model was "supported by Huawei Ascend," and revealed that after the mass production of the Ascend 950 supernode in the second half of the year, the price of the Pro version would be significantly reduced. This shift cannot be accomplished by simply modifying a few lines of code in the adaptation layer; it requires building a complete domestic computing infrastructure from scratch at the physical level. The trillion-parameter scale of V4 (pre-training data reaching 33 trillion tokens), coupled with the massive computing demands of millions of contexts, means you need tens of thousands of Ascend chips, data centers to accommodate them, power grids to supply these data centers, and maintenance teams to keep these machines running in sub-zero temperatures. Liang Wenfeng has taken the methodology from the world of bits to the world of atoms. Ultimately, computing power must take root in reinforced concrete and power transmission lines. On one hand, there are AI elites in plaid shirts coding and sipping hand-drip coffee in Silicon Valley; on the other hand, there are operations and maintenance personnel wrapped in military coats guarding data centers deep in the Inner Mongolian grasslands. This difference forms the backdrop for China's AI resistance to computing power blockade today. The transformation from a pure algorithm company to a "heavy asset" player with self-built data centers signifies that DeepSeek has bid farewell to the era of guerrilla warfare where "small efforts can achieve miracles" and has officially donned the armor of heavy infantry. The cost of this transformation is enormous; building data centers, buying chips, and laying network cables are all bottomless pits. More importantly, this heavy asset model means that operating costs will rise exponentially, while DeepSeek's commercial revenue remains extremely limited. This pricing strategy is essentially trading losses for an ecosystem, and free services for greater control over infrastructure. How long can a company that once rejected all giants and subsidized AI with its own money through quantitative trading survive in the face of this bottomless pit? A $20 billion compromise. In April, DeepSeek announced its first external funding round, targeting a valuation of 300 billion RMB (approximately $44 billion), planning to raise 50 billion RMB, including 30 billion RMB from external sources. Rumors of Tencent and Alibaba vying for entry were rampant. Many people believe that DeepSeek's funding is due to the high cost of building data centers. However, the core driving force behind DeepSeek's financing, besides purchasing graphics cards, is its "pure technical ideals," which proved vulnerable to the talent-grabbing tactics of tech giants. During the critical sprint of V4 development, major domestic companies launched a frenzied targeted poaching campaign against DeepSeek. From the second half of 2025 to the present, at least five core R&D members of DeepSeek have confirmed their departure. Wang Bingxuan, the core author of the first-generation model, went to Tencent; Luo Fuli, a core contributor to V3, was poached by Lei Jun to Xiaomi with a multi-million dollar annual salary; and Guo Daya, the core author of R1, joined ByteDance's Seed team. This is the most naked way a market economy operates. When your competitors have unlimited resources, while you insist on operating with your own funds, the talent market becomes your most vulnerable weakness. You can ask geniuses to take pay cuts and work overtime for the ideal of changing the world, but when a large company slams a check for millions in cash and stock options on the table, promising unlimited computing power, the pricing power of idealism is no longer in your hands. Liang Wenfeng's dilemma is actually the dilemma that every entrepreneur trying to build a "slow company" in China will encounter. In a market where large companies can buy anyone with money, the "no financing, no commercialization, just focus on technology" approach is extremely luxurious. Its cost is that you must accept that your team can be wiped out by your competitors at any time with money. This 300 billion valuation financing is not Liang Wenfeng's compromise with capital, but rather a redemption war he launched against major companies to preserve the V4 R&D team. He must sit at the capital table and use the same amount of real money to give those who remain a sufficient reason to stay. The potential entry of Tencent and Alibaba means that DeepSeek is no longer the solitary, purely technological idealist it once was. It has become a company with external shareholders and commercial pressure. The cost of this transformation is that Liang Wenfeng's once most prized "research freedom unaffected by external pressure" will inevitably be diluted. But he had no choice. When idealism is forced to don the armor of capital, where does the strength that sustains this massive machine's operation, that keeps the Ulanqab computer room roaring day and night, truly come from? Another kind of "miracle through sheer force"—the answer isn't in the algorithm, but in the power grid. Silicon Valley's biggest anxiety right now isn't a lack of chips, but a lack of electricity. Elon Musk is building a massive data center in Memphis, Tennessee; OpenAI is even discussing investing in nuclear power plants; and Microsoft has announced the restart of its Three Mile Island nuclear power plant in Pennsylvania to power its AI data centers. The ultimate power source for computing power is electricity—a stark and undeniable fact of physics. In the US, the electricity consumption of a large AI data center is equivalent to the daily electricity consumption of a medium-sized city. And the US power grid is an outdated network built in the 1950s, slow to expand, and fragmented regionally, unable to keep pace with the rapid expansion of computing power in the AI ​​era. What supports China's AI development in catching up with the US is not only the algorithm geniuses earning millions in annual salaries, but also the unsung heroes of ultra-high-voltage power transmission lines. The data center in Ulanqab was able to rise from the ground thanks to Inner Mongolia's abundant green electricity and China's world-leading power grid dispatching capabilities. Public data shows that Ulanqab has a green power installed capacity of 19.402 million kilowatts, accounting for approximately 65.9% of the total. Local low-cost green electricity is about 50% cheaper than in eastern regions. Coupled with an average annual temperature of only 4.3℃ and a natural cooling period of nearly 10 months, this allows for energy savings of 20% to 30%. When DeepSeek V4 runs, what truly fuels its growth is China's vast and extremely cheap power infrastructure. This is another dimension of "miracles through sheer force." Here is a very interesting and brutal historical contrast. In 1986, the US used the US-Japan Semiconductor Agreement to cripple Japan's semiconductor industry, forcing Japan to open its market and accept price controls. Japan's global semiconductor market share plummeted from 40% in 1986 to 15% in 2011. It took Japan thirty years to recover. Today, the United States is attempting to use the same logic to stifle China's AI, blocking chips, restricting computing power, and cutting off the technology supply chain. However, China's counterattack path is completely different from Japan's. Japan's failure stemmed from its semiconductor industry's heavy reliance on US technology licensing and market access; once cut off, it lost its ability to survive independently. China's AI counterattack, on the other hand, begins with rebuilding the most fundamental physical infrastructure: manufacturing its own chips, building its own data centers, establishing its own power grid, and open-sourcing its own models. This is an extremely cumbersome, extremely expensive, but also extremely difficult route to "strangle." While Silicon Valley is building magnificent Tower of Babel in the cloud, China is digging trenches in the ground. If the battle for computing power in the cloud is an extremely brutal war of attrition involving heavy assets, besides building server rooms and laying cables in Inner Mongolia, is there another way for us to escape cloud hegemony? Escaping the Cloud While Silicon Valley giants are building increasingly larger data centers, even planning computing power clusters worth hundreds of billions of dollars like OpenAI, China's counterattack has quietly shifted underground. The ultimate weapon against the US's computing power blockade isn't actually creating a chip more powerful than the H100, but rather stuffing large-scale models into everyone's mobile phone. Since we can't compete with heavy firepower in cloud data centers, let's bring the battlefield back to 1.4 billion smartphones and edge devices. This is a typical guerrilla warfare tactic, and one that's extremely difficult to block. You can ban the export of high-end GPUs, but you can't confiscate the mobile phone in every Chinese person's pocket. In 2026, amidst the computing power anxiety triggered by DeepSeek, Chinese mobile phone manufacturers Xiaomi, OPPO, and vivo began a frantic "edge-side migration." They're no longer content with simply using a phone as a display for calling cloud APIs. Instead, through extreme model distillation and compression, they've crammed a miniaturized super brain into a domestically produced phone costing only a few thousand yuan. The core of this technical approach is "distillation." Simply put, it involves using a super-large model (the teacher) to train a small model (the student), allowing the small model to learn the teacher's "thinking methods" rather than rote memorization of all the teacher's "knowledge." Through extreme distillation and quantization compression, a large model that originally required hundreds of GPUs to run is compressed to only 1.2GB to 2.5GB, running smoothly on a single mobile chip. Mobile AI applications like MNN Chat already allow users to run DeepSeek R1 distilled models locally on their phones. The significance of this edge AI is that you don't need a constant 5G connection, nor do you need to pay a $100 monthly subscription fee to Silicon Valley giants. The large model is in your pocket, it can run offline, and you don't need to spend a penny on cloud computing power.

Since I can't afford to build a super boiler room for centralized heating, I'll just give every household a small stove.

Of course, edge AI is not perfect.

Limited by the computing power and memory of mobile phone chips, the capabilities of edge-side models are far less than those of massive cloud-based models. It can help you write an email, translate a text, or summarize an article, but if you want it to derive a complex mathematical theorem or analyze a legal contract hundreds of pages long, it will fall short. But this is enough. Because for most ordinary people, the AI ​​they need is never a super brain capable of deriving mathematical theorems, but rather a "personal assistant" to help them handle daily tasks. When large models become extremely cheap, even pocket-sized, how will they change those forgotten corners of Silicon Valley? Digital Equality in the Global South If you were sitting in a panoramic glass office in Manhattan, you'd probably think that the $100 price increase for GPT-5.5 is worthwhile, because it can help you write a perfect M&A financial report in a second. But if you were standing in a cornfield in Uganda, East Africa, facing crops withered and yellow due to climate anomalies, no one could afford the $100 subscription fee, because the average monthly income in Uganda is less than $150. Silicon Valley giants are discussing how to rule the world with AI, while farmers in Uganda and poor students in Southeast Asia are stepping into the digital age for the first time thanks to DeepSeek's open-source nature. GPT-5.5 serves those who can afford it, and its corpus is almost entirely in English. If you ask it a question in Swahili or Javanese, it not only provides a halting response but also consumes several times more tokens than in English. Silicon Valley giants have actively abandoned these peripheral markets due to "low commercial returns." Meanwhile, China's open-source model has become the digital infrastructure of the Global South. In Uganda, the local NGO Sunbird AI, using the Sunflower system fine-tuned based on the Chinese open-source model Qwen, expanded the number of supported local languages ​​from 6 to 31. This system is now deployed in the Ugandan government's agricultural extension system, sending planting advice to farmers in Swahili. In Malaysia, tech companies have fine-tuned AI models using an open-source platform to comply with Sharia law, supporting not only Malay and Indonesian languages ​​but also ensuring that output content conforms to the religious and cultural standards of the Muslim market. From Indonesia's digital identity system to Kenya's Swahili-language medical Q&A, Chinese technology is penetrating the underlying social structures of these countries. Data released in early 2026 by OpenRouter, the world's largest AI model API aggregation platform, showed that Chinese AI models consumed more tokens on the platform than their American competitors for the first time. In one statistical week, the top 10 most popular models globally consumed a total of 8.7 trillion tokens, with Chinese models accounting for approximately 61%. Open source has broken the US monopoly on AI discourse, enabling resource-scarce developing countries to bridge the digital divide. This is not some grand narrative of a US-China rivalry; this is the true "rural-to-urban" strategy in the AI ​​era. China's open-source AI strategy is objectively becoming an extremely effective form of "soft power" output. As Silicon Valley giants erect high walls in the cloud, attempting to become the digital landlords of the new era, those "tech refugees" who cannot afford the rent have finally found their own spark in the soil of open source and edge computing. Technology should never be an unattainable luxury. Silicon Valley has built exquisitely designed apartments, with strict access control, open only to VIPs. But we have built a water pipe connecting every household. This water pipe begins in a server room in Inner Mongolia at -20 degrees Celsius, amidst the roar of ultra-high-voltage power lines, and in a war of valuations reaching 300 billion. Every step of it was heavy, expensive, and filled with coercion and compromise. Liang Wenfeng once wanted to create a purely technology company, but reality forced him to build data centers, raise funds, and compete with large companies for talent. He had no choice, because he chose a more difficult path: not to make AI a luxury, but to make it a tap. And the end of this water pipe is in a domestically produced mobile phone costing a few thousand yuan, in the rough fingers of a Ugandan farmer, and in the lives of every ordinary person yearning to bridge the digital divide. No matter how high the walls of computing power are built, they cannot stop the tap water flowing downhill.

data.title: AAVE fell down, SPK ate his fill
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 19, 2026, KelpDAO suffered an attack due to a cross-chain bridge vulnerability, resulting in a loss of approximately $292 million. The impact of this attack continues to unfold: an epic capital migration is taking place in the DeFi lending sector. AAVE is experiencing a massive capital flight, while Spark is absorbing a continuous influx of liquidity.

Since the lending leader AAVE encountered a black swan crisis, its token has plummeted, its TVL has shrunk dramatically, and massive amounts of capital have fled in panic; while Spark Protocol and its native token SPK, backed by the MakerDAO (now Sky) ecosystem, have continuously absorbed liquidity, with their price soaring and locked-in value doubling. It can be said that when AAVE falls, SPK reaps the rewards. The DeFi lending sector is undergoing a complete transformation.

I. AAVE's Fall

On April 18, KelpDAO's rsETH cross-chain bridge based on LayerZero suffered a vulnerability attack. Hackers maliciously minted fake rsETH collateral without real asset backing and deposited it into the AAVE protocol to borrow a large amount of native WETH, directly causing AAVE a potential bad debt shortfall of nearly $200 million, breaking the record for the largest DeFi security incident this year. For details, please see "From KelpDAO to Aave Bad Debts: How Cross-Chain Vulnerabilities Pierce the DeFi Ecosystem"

A large amount of funds fled AAVE: Justin Sun retrieved his 65,584 ETH ($154 million) on Aave; Abraxas Capital withdrew $392 million; MEXC withdrew $431 million; whale 0x7CD0 withdrew $405.7 million… According to DeFiLlama data, AAVE TVL has plummeted from $26.396 billion on April 18 to $14.294 billion as of press time, a drop of 45.85%. The reason: Damaged collateral triggered market freezes, liquidation halts, and forced deleveraging, forcing users to withdraw funds or liquidate positions. The price of AAVE token has fallen 21.13% from its high of $118.65 on April 18 to $93.58 as of press time. On April 23, Aave released the latest updates on the rsETH security incident on the X platform. It disclosed that it has suspended rsETH reserve-related operations on the Ethereum mainnet and networks such as Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea to prevent the withdrawal of excess aETH rsETH, thereby pushing the position close to the 95% liquidation threshold. This measure aims to preserve as much capital as possible and reduce systemic risk during the asset recovery process. Aave stated that it will continue to disclose further progress and disposal plans to the community. On April 24th, Aave founder Stani Kulechov stated that Aave is his life's work, and the team is fully committed to addressing the current risk events. He announced a personal contribution of 5,000 ETH to participate in the DeFi United joint rescue plan. This plan aims to unite industry partners to fill collateral gaps, stabilize the market, and promote the implementation of more commitments to restore normal market operations as soon as possible. Other platforms also offered assistance. Lido Finance proposed in its governance forum to provide a one-time injection of up to 2,500 stETH to the Aave-led rsETH rescue plan to fill collateral gaps and reduce the risk of spillover into the DeFi ecosystem. Bybit-backed Mantle proposed in its governance forum to provide a loan of 30,000 ETH to Aave to cover the bad debt risk caused by the recent attack.

The EtherFi Foundation proposed injecting 5,000 ETH into the rsETH rescue pool to cover collateral shortfalls, protect user assets, and prevent bad debts from spreading within the DeFi system.

According to on-chain analyst Ember, these donations have covered 43,500 ETH in losses (rsETH's total loss was 68,900 ETH). This has indeed had a significant effect on restoring confidence in Aave: funds have stopped flowing out of Aave.

II. SPK Takes the Benefit

Spark, the Ethereum-based non-custodial lending protocol, has become the biggest beneficiary of the Kelp DAO hack.

In the past few days, the TVL of the Spark protocol and the price of the SPK token have surged, making it the first winner in the restructuring of the DeFi lending sector.

According to DeFiLlama data, Spark TVL was only $3.727 billion on April 19th, but has surged to $5.084 billion as of press time, an increase of 36.41%. The SPK token price once rose to a high of $0.06234. As of press time, it has retreated somewhat, trading at $0.04482, a 7-day increase of 92.4%.

TVL measures the total USD value of all user deposits in a protocol's smart contract. For lending platforms like Spark, it tracks the total value of collateral provided by borrowers and the total value of liquidity provided by lenders in their markets.

Protocol TVL spikes are typically triggered by one of two scenarios: either an activity to attract liquidity, or funds are actively rotating from competing platforms. Spark falls into the latter category.

A significant portion of the TVL growth comes from individual wallets: according to on-chain analyst Ai Yi, Justin Sun has deposited a total of $1.3 billion in assets into Spark, with USDS Farming rewards reaching $5.38 million.

Spark's decisive victory in this hacking attack stemmed from its proactive risk management capabilities. On January 29th, the Spark governance forum formally proposed removing rsETH from the whitelist and halting new supply access; on the same day, Aave launched the rsETH staking market. Faced with risk, the market voted with its feet. Large amounts of capital fled AAVE, whose risk control was questionable, and flowed into Spark, which had stricter risk controls.

Spark co-founder Sam MacPherson recently published two articles regarding the hacking incident, specifically mentioning that "Spark's core lies in institutional-level risk management": "Spark (and Sky) prioritizes long-term development, not short-term TVL growth. Collateral re-collateralization (or 'circular collateralization') on SparkLend is limited to ETH and Lido wstETH, reflecting an intentional limitation on recursive risk rather than yield optimization. Overly relaxing the restrictions on 'circular collateralization' would introduce hidden correlations and liquidity dependencies, thereby increasing system vulnerability."

Furthermore, Spark's backing by MakerDAO is an advantage that AAVE does not possess.

Specifically, Spark is the first sub-DAO under MakerDAO's "Endgame Strategy," officially launched in May 2024. It has its own governance token, SPK, but key decisions (such as risk frameworks and asset whitelists) still require Maker governance approval. There is a strong technical and financial bond between the two: Maker can directly mint DAI/USDS and inject Spark at zero cost and without barriers, providing billions of stablecoins of liquidity—a privilege not available to Aave/Compound; Spark enjoys exclusive credit lines granted by Maker, enabling it to lend DAI at rates far below market rates, forming a core competitive advantage; Spark's collateral access, parameters, and risk control rules are uniformly set by Maker governance. Conclusion: The fall of AAVE and the rise of SPK represent a highly representative security reshuffling event in the DeFi industry. AAVE is gradually stabilizing its situation with support from various industry stakeholders, demonstrating the resilience of a veteran giant; Spark is rising rapidly, but will also face long-term operational challenges after the liquidity boom ends.

Appendix: Spark co-founder Sam MacPherson's article "Spark Security Framework: A Comprehensive Overview"

Spark (@sparkdotfi) has been rapidly evolving, continuously adding new security features. We believe now is a good time to release a comprehensive overview of Spark's risk management capabilities.

Spark Savings

Spark Savings is a non-custodial savings vault where users can deposit stablecoins (such as USDT, USDC, USDS, etc.) to earn on-chain yields.

Funding Backing

All Spark Savings USD deposits are backed 1:1 by USDS. Spark Savings enjoys equal priority with USDS, and every deposit is supported by Sky's full financial strength.
