All Spark Savings USD deposits are backed by USDS's full financial strength.

All Spark Savings USD **First Loss Capital** USDS (and Spark Savings) has a multi-tiered loss protection mechanism, starting from the Prime tier. **Tier 1 – Internal Subprime Risk Capital (Prime Tier):** Subprime risk capital is the first line of defense in the allocation system for absorbing investment losses. Each Prime is responsible for holding subprime capital in its vault corresponding to its risk-weighted allocation ratio, serving as the first line of defense in the event of allocation losses. Spark is well-capitalized, with over $35 million in stablecoin equity capital. **Tier 2 – External Subprime Risk Capital from the Main Fund:** The main fund can obtain additional subprime risk capital from other main funds. This risk capital has the same priority as the main fund's internal subprime risk capital and helps to compensate for losses caused by the main fund's asset allocation and risk exposure. **Tier 3 – External Senior Risk Capital (srUSDS):** This feature is planned for deployment soon. External senior risk capital is provided by the srUSDS smart contract, through which users can provide USDS to Sky Core as senior risk capital. Senior risk capital will only begin absorbing losses after the secondary risk capital has been 100% depleted. Layer 4 - Surplus Buffer (Internal Senior Risk Capital): The Sky Protocol Buffer (also known as the Surplus Buffer) is an accumulation of stability fees and liquidation penalties held by the protocol, used to cover bad debts in the event of remaining losses. Layer 5 - Comprehensive Surplus Buffer: Once the Surplus Buffer is depleted, losses will be covered by Sky's Comprehensive Surplus Buffer. This buffer allows Sky to utilize a portion of other Prime internal secondary risk capital pre-injected by Sky to address significant loss events. Layer 6 - Token Backup: If losses exceed the above risk capital sources, Sky will mint SKY tokens to replenish protocol capital and cover any remaining bad debts. Fair Loss Sharing: Any remaining losses will only be equally distributed among all USDS holders, including the Spark Savings stablecoin vault (fully backed by USDS), once all other secondary risk capital sources and token reserves have been exhausted. Sky's multi-tiered capital reserve mechanism provides Spark Savings vault users with a high degree of protection against losses. Combined, Spark Savings vaults have hundreds of millions of dollars in loss and event protection. Liquidity: Spark Savings vaults offer industry-leading instant liquidity, making them ideal for institutional users. The Spark Savings USDT vault has an instant liquidity buffer of over 400 million USDT available for redemptions; while the Spark Savings USDC vault, through integration with Sky PSM, can handle billions of dollars in redemptions. Savings vault contracts maintain a liquidity buffer of up to $10 million to meet typical withdrawal volumes through atomic redemptions. For larger withdrawals, Spark offers an asynchronous liquidity intent mechanism, allowing users to sign withdrawal requests of any amount, which are then quickly completed through the Spark liquidity layer; in most cases, large withdrawal requests can be completed within 1 minute (5 Ethereum blocks).

Transparency and Third-Party Ratings

Spark Savings Vault boasts industry-leading transparency in its supported assets and related allocation strategies. You can access real-time data from Spark and Sky through various open resources, including:

Spark Data Dashboard: data.spark.fi

Sky Information Dashboard: info.skyeco.com

Spark App: app.spark.fi

In addition, Spark has received ratings for Spark Savings products from Credora, a leading independent cryptocurrency-native risk rating agency. These ratings are linked to the Spark App, and the full report can be viewed on the Credora website here.

Transparency and Third-Party Ratings

Spark Savings Vault offers industry-leading transparency in its supported assets and related allocation strategies.

Spark Savings Vault offers industry-leading transparency in its supported assets and related allocation strategies.

Spark App: app.spark.fi

In addition, Spark has received ratings for Spark Savings products from Credora, a leading independent cryptocurrency-native risk rating agency. These ratings are linked to the Spark App, and the full report can be viewed here on the Credora website.

Event Response

If the Spark Savings Vault is at risk of loss, Spark can put the Vault into recovery mode to mitigate the risk. Temporarily suspending withdrawals ensures fair treatment for all users and prevents a run on the Vault.

Future Improvements

We are working on more improvements and features to further enhance the security of the Spark Savings Vault, including:

Adding primary risk capital coverage at the Sky tier

Redundant withdrawal functionality

Ratings and reviews from leading trading financial risk experts

First Loss Capital

Spark and Sky are implementing a first loss capital Vault (as described above, located at tier 3 of the capital stack), enabling users to earn higher returns by committing funds to support the protocol in the event of potential losses.

Spark will launch a permissionless withdrawal mechanism to ensure that savings depositors can withdraw their principal immediately at any time; even in extreme cases such as unexpected Spark infrastructure outages, user assets can still be withdrawn normally. The team is actively engaging with leading institutions in the traditional financial sector to obtain more institutional-level risk assessments and credit rating certifications. This will further demonstrate to users that Spark Savings Vault operates strictly in accordance with the highest standards of security and risk management. SparkLend is Spark's permissionless money market. Compared to similar platforms, SparkLend has always operated conservatively, employing a streamlined collateral portfolio, multi-oracle pricing, strict interest rate limits, and first-loss capital. The rsETH incident reminds us that these pillars are not isolated. Their design aims to stack with each other, thus preventing a cascading effect from the failure of any single component (oracle, issuer, liquidator, market liquidity) leading to bad debts.

Current Risk Architecture

Restricted Collateral Portfolio

SparkLend deliberately restricts the types of collateral. The ETH electronic mode is limited to wstETH and rETH. The BTC electronic mode is being completely removed: this change has been publicly announced on the Sky forum, tentatively scheduled to take effect on June 4th, with remaining positions to be forcibly liquidated on June 8th. The affected asset size is currently small (only one borrower with approximately $1.6 million, plus a small number of smaller positions), and the removal process will be carried out through a public timeline, rather than a sudden change in parameters.

Minimum Recollateralization

Collateral provided to SparkLend reserves will remain in the reserves and will not be redeployed to external strategies.

Minimum Recollateralization

Rate Limiting All cross-module fund inflows and outflows in SparkLend are rate-limited at the smart contract level: deposits, withdrawals, cross-chain bridging, and PSM swaps are all configured with rate limits. Furthermore, Spark's allocation system enforces debt caps and minimum/maximum inventory ranges for each market. A single depositor or a single adverse event will not deplete the protocol's funds within a single block; rate limiting restricts the maximum risk capital faced by any single path per unit of time. Three-Oracle Median Pricing is aggregated using the median from three oracles, which is obtained from RedStone, Chainlink, and Chronicle. The median is used when all three oracles return valid and not expired values. When only two oracles return valid values, their average is used. Additionally, a single-oracle fallback mechanism exists. As a result, the failure or damage of a single oracle will not cause SparkLend price fluctuations. **Pegged Asset Termination Oracle** For collateral traded at hard-coded or exchange rate prices (e.g., wstETH, rETH, weETH, cbBTC, WBTC, LBTC), the peg ratio oracle continuously compares the market price of an asset to its underlying asset. When the discrepancy exceeds a threshold for each asset, a termination switch stops new lending on SparkLend, preventing users from posting bad collateral at outdated "face value" prices and leaving with healthy debt. **Programmatic Liquidity Injection** SparkLend's liquidity buffer is not fixed. The Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) automatically injects and drains USDS, USDC, and USDT from SparkLend based on target lending rates, utilization, and available inventory on other exchanges. If SparkLend utilization is too high, the SLL replenishes idle liquidity to ensure smooth withdrawals and liquidations. Idle funds flow to other exchanges that offer higher risk-adjusted yields. This is the significance of Spark as the largest depository in its market: liquidity is allocated based on demand, not just usage.

Planned Improvements

Continuous Collateral Risk Review

We are conducting a comprehensive review of every collateral asset in SparkLend, covering not only the individual risk profile of each asset but also its dependencies (issuer, custodian, oracle data, secondary market liquidity, and redemption paths). This process will transition to a continuous review framework to ensure that collateral risk is continuously monitored and updated as circumstances change.

Oracle Improvements

We are developing a more progressive oracle design that defaults to hard-coded or exchange rate pricing under normal circumstances, only switching to market pricing when persistent discrepancies are observed. The goal is to give the protocol a faster, more automated response to truly structurally de-anchored events while maintaining the existing mechanisms' protection against flash crashes and oracle outages, thus enabling orderly liquidation and preventing bad debts from accumulating behind stagnant prices.

We are developing a more progressive oracle design that defaults to hard-coded or exchange rate pricing under normal circumstances, only switching to market pricing when persistent discrepancies are observed.

We are developing a more progressive oracle design that defaults to hard-coded or exchange rate pricing under normal circumstances, only switching to market pricing when persistent discrepancies are observed.

We are developing a more progressive oracle design that defaults to hard-coded or exchange rate outages.

We are developing a more progressive oracle design that defaults to hard-coded or exchange rate outages, while maintaining the existing mechanisms' protection against flash crashes and oracle outage This design aims to complement, rather than replace, existing circuit breaker mechanisms: oracles automatically handle ongoing market volatility, while circuit breakers serve as a last resort in the event of catastrophic failures. Faster Market Parameter Updates Currently, most parameter changes in SparkLend require a full governance process, resulting in delays of several days. This is acceptable for steady-state adjustments, but too slow for tail-risk events. Current work involves delegating a limited set of risk parameters to risk managers to perform actions such as tightening the loan-to-value ratio (LTV), lowering the supply cap, or adjusting the interest rate model within hours, while the final decision-making power remains with the Spark and Sky governance bodies. Spark Independent Markets Aggregated lending markets offer a better user experience but also have limitations. Spark also provides independent markets through Morpho for handling collateral with unique risk characteristics. Independent lending allows you to price risk more effectively and remove collateral that no longer provides a good risk-adjusted yield. In addition to risk instruments, Spark also uses independent markets for any non-Ethereum on-chain lending. This allows Spark to connect to exchanges and fintech platforms without deploying or maintaining its own infrastructure. Improvements Spark will prioritize markets with advanced oracle systems that can withstand the failure of any single provider. Spark will add user interface support so users can participate in on-chain lending directly through the Spark App. Spark Liquidity Layer The Spark Liquidity Layer (SLL) operates as Spark's non-custodial capital allocator in the DeFi/CeFi and TradeFi space. SLL has been operating stably since November 2024. The core design of SLL is to ensure that capital flows are constrained, predictable, and bounded under any circumstances, including periods of market stress. A key security feature of SLL is that Spark governance must pre-configure approved trading venues and set strict interest rate limits. Automated wallets can only transfer funds between these pre-approved trading venues at prescribed interest rate limits. These constraints ensure that funds do not rapidly drain from any single trading venue, and that changes in fund allocation are gradual, rather than reactive adjustments under stress. This directly addresses a core failure mode observed in recent market events: unrestrained capital flows lead to rapid liquidity depletion and trigger cascading stress in the market. SLL's threat model assumes that even if automated wallets are completely compromised, the protocol faces no substantial risk. Even under this assumption, funds are still confined to pre-defined venue and interest rate limits, ensuring that no single component introduces unlimited risk into the system. Improvements This approach extends beyond design to proactive risk management and asset allocation decisions: As part of a broader de-risking initiative, Spark has deprecated many markets. Spark will continue to proactively manage risk as yield opportunities are consolidated. Spark will remove support for all Aave markets from the SLL whitelist. Although all Aave funds were removed shortly after the rsETH incident, the ability to deposit Aave funds again will also be discontinued.

Leveraging AI for automation to detect broader DeFi events and take appropriate action.

Bridging

Currently, there are 2 bridges online in the Sky/Spark ecosystem.

SkyLink: Official Sky Governance and Token Bridge

SkyLink connects Sky governance and cross-chain USDS. The governance configuration is 4/7 DVN, providing high decentralization and redundancy. The token bridge requires 2/2 DVN.

SkyLink is deployed on Solana and Avalanche.

SkyLink has recently deployed a robust configuration, but we are taking additional defensive measures to address increasingly complex state actors.

Improvements

In partnership with LayerZero, the amount of DVN on the token bridge is expected to increase from 2/2.

Spark Governance Bridge (Avalanche)

Spark runs its own LayerZero governance bridge to support Spark Savings USDC on Avalanche. This bridge is currently 2/2 but is planned for upgrade in the coming weeks to match SkyLink's 4/7 configuration.

No associated token bridge, and very little venture capital (approximately $2 million).

data.title: Hyperliquid teaches us that vertical integration is the key to competition.
data.description:
Author: Joel John Source: X, @Decentralisedco Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

How Hyperliquid, USDAI, MetaMask, Maple, and Centrifuge build industry moats through vertical integration and practical value.

Looking at the total revenue of $3.5 billion in the decentralized sector, a clear industry pattern has emerged. Approximately 40% of the revenue comes from derivatives trading platforms, with Hyperliquid alone generating approximately $902 million. The second largest revenue stream is decentralized exchanges, with the leading project Uniswap generating approximately $927 million in fees. The lending sector follows closely behind, with platforms like MakerDAO generating nearly $500 million in revenue.

These leading projects share a common core attribute: they are all capital-intensive businesses.

These types of tracks cannot be quickly implemented through short-term code development alone; they require long-term patience, deep collaboration with capital, and investors willing to bear the platform's potential risks. Some leading protocols rely on complex supply-side resource networks to gradually build insurmountable competitive barriers. Take Jupiter as an example; its massive ecosystem integration system is something that competitors cannot replicate in the short term. The core value of blockchain lies in enabling fund transfers and verifying whether transactions conform to the rules set by developers. Therefore, blockchain can only unleash its true value when it undertakes capital-intensive businesses. Perpetual contract exchanges allow massive amounts of capital to circulate repeatedly within a single day, continuously generating returns; lending platforms, on the other hand, extract a share of the interest generated by these massive assets, ensuring stable profits. For example, Aave generated approximately $920 million in asset interest over the past year, earning about $123 million in returns. However, for such capital aggregation platforms to maintain a long-term market monopoly, they must firmly control three core elements: liquidity supply, user demand, and comprehensive distribution channels. Hyperliquid is a unique benchmark in this field. Although it has paid nearly $100 million in developer code incentives, the vast majority of its revenue still comes from its official native front-end channels. The platform can both retain core high-value users and continuously expand access points for new users, achieving two-way growth of the ecosystem. What is the underlying logic of this business model? On the surface, Hyperliquid appears to be just an ordinary supply-side capital aggregator, but its core value goes far beyond that. In the Web3 space, traffic distribution is inherently a business with high barriers to entry. Top protocols often manage to retain and lock in core users. A comparison of the revenue gap between decentralized exchanges and on-chain order aggregators clearly illustrates this point. Hyperliquid's total revenue is approximately $1.1 billion, with developer collaborations accounting for only about 6%. Fox Wallet, deeply integrated into the Ethereum ecosystem, earned $184 million in transaction fees last year from its token exchange business; Solana's ecosystem wallet, Phantom, also generated nearly $180 million in revenue. And this is only a small fraction of their vast ecosystem economies. Traffic-driven retail products built on a single leading protocol can only operate sustainably if backed by sufficient liquidity and active economic activity, and possessing the ability to transfer value. Vertically integrated capital aggregation platforms, with their deep liquidity reserves, naturally possess a core advantage in attracting and retaining users. Examining the industry from a new perspective reveals that capital in the crypto market is no longer a standardized, homogeneous commodity, but rather a core necessity for the ecosystem's operation. Vertical capital integration maximizes user retention; within a closed-loop ecosystem, continuous capital circulation generates massive liquidity, enabling efficient reuse of funds and continuous value generation. Capital is never a moat, but rather a byproduct of vertical integration; vertical integration itself is the true core barrier, and capital is merely a byproduct. Simply relying on high TVL (Total Value Locked) assets cannot guarantee long-term success. Idle, inefficiently utilized capital can become a huge vulnerability for a platform if attacked by hackers. This explains why major protocols are currently focusing on niche scenarios and building differentiated economic output capabilities to construct core competitiveness. 1. According to official data from USDAI, the operator behind $CHIP, nearly $100 million in loans have been disbursed this quarter, with another $1.5 billion in financing projects underway; its high-risk asset-backed tiered products offer an annualized return of approximately 16%. 2. Maple Finance's high-risk liquidity pool offers a stable annualized return of 15%–20%, matching or even exceeding the current 12.6% annualized interest rate of Aave's USDC liquidity pool. The platform aggregates a massive number of high-quality borrowers, leveraging ample liquidity to continuously create value for the real economy. 3. Hyperliquid is a prime example of efficient capital reuse. In the past year, its revenue reached $942 million, with an average daily locked-up asset value of approximately $3.5 billion. A rough calculation shows that every $1 of assets on the platform circulates 285 times annually, generating $0.30 in transaction fees per unit of locked-up assets. In contrast, on the Aave lending market, every $1 of locked-up assets only generates $0.05 in transaction fees, a significant difference. Admittedly, comparing Hyperliquid in the exchange sector with Aave in the lending sector involves different scenarios. However, in the crypto market, where users have free choice and brand loyalty is weak, capital will always flow to the most profitable and efficient sectors. At the same time, considering potential risks such as hacking attacks, investors will also demand higher yield premiums for high-risk sectors. Currently, perpetual contract exchanges are the only core sector capable of activating idle capital and continuously generating fee revenue through high-frequency on-chain reuse. The Vertical Integration Wave in the Crypto Industry [Image of a PNG file] More and more protocols are implementing vertical integration strategies. In the Web3 field, without a vertically integrated system to lower the barriers to collaboration, capital providers will only become standardized resources that can be easily replaced. Only by achieving deep ecosystem integration and creating a unique closed-loop experience can user loyalty be built. Maple's core competitive advantage stems from its years of experience serving hedge funds and market makers; Centrifuge, an off-chain real-world asset protocol, is deeply integrated with institutions, securing nearly $1 billion in bond funding from Janus Henderson Asset Management through Grove Finance this year alone. These projects no longer exploit fragmented ecosystem needs but instead achieve vertical integration across the entire value chain, creating superior integrated products for end-users and building a robust competitive moat that competitors cannot easily replicate. In an era where capital and connections are scarce core competitive advantages, Maple's risk control and underwriting experience, and Centrifuge's credible capital coordination capabilities, are irreplaceable core competencies. Companies pursuing vertical integration often proactively outsource some business processes to third parties, primarily because self-operation is extremely inefficient. Compared to high-yield businesses like token swaps and credit underwriting, self-developed custody services and issuing physical bank cards offer very limited profit margins. However, when a company enters a phase of exponential growth, full-chain self-control is the key to building a long-term competitive advantage, which is also the core logic of the industry's wave of mergers and acquisitions. When a company completes deep vertical integration, its competitive dimension is no longer limited to a single product, but rather a comprehensive closed-loop user experience. Take Hyperliquid's upcoming HIP-4 proposal as an example: users can deposit and withdraw funds for free through Native Markets, open prediction market positions with one click, and use their holdings as collateral to directly trade perpetual contracts. The entire process is seamlessly integrated based on a self-developed risk engine; even in the traditional financial system, it would be difficult to achieve similar operations without the intervention of professional investment banks. Real-world community examples vividly demonstrate the practical value of ecosystem vertical integration: User sharing: Originally, I thought transferring funds from Hyperliquid to Interactive Brokers (IBKR) via USDH would be cumbersome, but in reality, it only took 10 minutes to complete the binding and initiate a test transfer, and the funds arrived smoothly on the same day. Native Markets officially announced the launch of Hyperliquid's zero-fee deposit and withdrawal service, seamlessly integrated with USDH. Hyperliquid boasts core advantages such as self-custody, deep liquidity, zero-gas-fee transfers, and stable trading around the clock. From deposit channels, risk control systems, trading interfaces, and underlying liquidity to the issuance of the platform's native token, Hyperliquid achieves full-chain self-control. New competitors must compete head-on in six core sectors to rival it, a very high barrier to entry. For new projects, leveraging a mature, integrated ecosystem to capture a niche market is far more valuable than starting from scratch on entirely new public chains like Monad. Data shows that Monad's five perpetual contract protocols have only accumulated $2.6 billion in derivatives trading volume. Closed-loop integrated ecosystems, exemplified by Hyperliquid, can continuously attract developers, expand ecosystem partnerships, and gain industry attention, while simultaneously protecting the rights of token holders. Major exchanges have long recognized this industry trend. Coinbase acquired Deribit, a leader in derivatives, built its own custody business, jointly issued the USDC stablecoin with Circle to generate revenue from reserve assets, established wallet infrastructure and user deposit channels covering over 100 countries, and even developed its own public chain, fully promoting vertical integration across the entire value chain. However, Coinbase's early focus on the retail user sector was somewhat ahead of its time; ordinary users do not have a demand for on-chain content creation or decentralized social networking. While its ecosystem integration strategy has been implemented, its development is limited by layers of regulatory barriers, internal processes, and strategic priorities. This is precisely the core difference between open integrated ecosystems and closed, walled-garden ecosystems. Leading exchanges with a market capitalization of $60 billion naturally lack the motivation to serve small and medium-sized developers. In contrast, Hyperliquid, while refining its core trading experience, also improves its supporting ecosystem, continuously empowering its native token and creating a positive value cycle. In this model, the platform token serves as the value link and underlying consensus carrier of the entire vertically integrated system, maintaining ecosystem operation and value accumulation. This also clarifies two commonly confused concepts in the industry: tokenized protocols and tokenized business entities. The core of tokenization protocols is to lower the barrier to entry for third-party developers and enhance token value through ecosystem empowerment, commonly achieved through market buybacks and burns. While centralized business giants like Coinbase and Robinhood possess strong economic power, they cannot replicate Hyperliquid's self-developed, self-governed, and user-governed core network system. Hyperliquid's airdrop mechanism precisely selects ecosystem contributors, deeply aligning token holders with the platform's interests and encouraging them to actively contribute to the ecosystem. The platform even uses 99% of its revenue for token buybacks in the secondary market. Analogous to traditional listed companies, this is equivalent to a company using all its revenue for employee stock buybacks; this distribution model is reshaping the industry's value logic. Regardless of ideological differences within the industry, commercial realities will ultimately dictate the direction of development: Solana emphasizes immutability, Ethereum focuses on censorship resistance and open-source spirit, but the flow of capital and traffic already indicates that the industry is compromising with commercial realities. Coexisting with Chaos The ultimate vertical integration across the entire value chain inevitably requires a moderate concession to the ideal of complete decentralization in exchange for economies of scale. The internet industry has long proven this pattern: in the 1990s, the public envisioned an unrestrained and unregulated open internet, but ultimately, chaos forced the establishment of rules and constraints. Legal scholar Tim Wu, in his book *Who Controls the Internet*, deeply analyzes that the development trajectory of all commercial networks is a gradual transition from absolute freedom and complete decentralization to a balanced model of rule constraints to adapt to the needs of business collaboration. Appropriately weakening the original ideal and supplementing the rule system is necessary to achieve large-scale commercial expansion; blindly pursuing absolute freedom will only breed industry chaos. The current rapid expansion and rule improvement of the encryption industry are highly similar to the Western Development Strategy and the internet bubble era; after a period of unbridled growth, a period of standardized convergence is inevitable. This trend offers clear insights for entrepreneurs: Referencing the profit data of Little Fox Wallet and Phantom Wallet, the revenue of these downstream ecosystem products far exceeds that of most Layer 2 public chains. Underlying infrastructure divorced from actual needs and isolated exchanges without traffic support have long lost their commercial viability; potential risks such as hacker attacks will further amplify operational crises. Focusing on products built upon current liquidity and user bases is the key to survival. While replicating mature vertically integrated products in the short term is unrealistic, entrepreneurs can leverage leading ecosystems for secondary development and growth. The iterative history of operating systems follows a similar pattern: after BlackBerry's decline and iOS's monopoly, developers inevitably concentrate on high-traffic areas; the crypto industry is replicating this trend, and high capital incentives will further prolong the consolidation period of the sector. I often cite Kuburn Park in Bangalore as an example: the park's cleanliness, orderliness, and long-term stable operation over the years are largely due to clear rules and regulations—limited closing times, prohibition of illegal gatherings, etc. Rules may be stringent, but they are the foundation for the efficient operation of public spaces. The same applies to internet platforms and blockchain protocols; while restrictive rules may not be popular, they are the cornerstone of a stable ecosystem. In the era of vertical integration, users will proactively accept reasonable rules to ensure the security of their assets and maintain the continued growth of the crypto ecosystem they participate in long-term. Stablecoins, real-world assets RWAs, closed-loop risk-controlled perpetual contracts, professional lending market makers, off-chain query protocols like DeriveXYZ… all these sectors point to the same ultimate trend: vertically integrated capital aggregation platforms are proactively relinquishing their ideal of complete decentralization, making appropriate trade-offs to ensure the long-term development and commercialization of the industry.
